In training aircraft, superchargers are more common (simpler, more predictable). High-altitude or high-performance aircraft often use turbos for efficiency and power recovery.
exam_tip: Supercharger: mechanical, instant, parasitic loss. Turbo: exhaust-driven, lag, efficient. Know trade-offs.
key_facts: Supercharger: belt-driven, instant response, power loss
Turbo: exhaust-driven, lag, recovers waste energy
Both increase air density and power
Supercharger simpler, turbo more efficient
Turbo requires intercooler for heat management
Different training applications
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the basic principle of operation of an exhaust driven turbocharger.
concept: Exhaust gases exiting the cylinders have significant thermal and kinetic energy. A turbocharger captures that energy. The hot exhaust passes through a turbine (similar to a small jet engine turbine) causing it to spin at very high RPM—up to 150,000+ RPM. This turbine is mechanically linked on the same shaft to a compressor that draws intake air and compresses it before sending it to the engine. The compressed air (boost) has higher density and pressure, so more oxygen's available per unit volume for combustion. Higher oxygen means the engine can burn more fuel and produce more power. Unlike a supercharger, the turbo doesn't draw power from the engine; it uses what would otherwise be wasted exhaust energy. The system's quite efficient. The downside: turbo lag from throttle application until the turbine spins up to speed, and high exhaust temperatures requiring cooling.
exam_tip: Exhaust spins turbine → turbine spins compressor → compressor pressurises intake air → more power. Simple concept, elegant system.
key_facts: Exhaust drives turbine at very high RPM
Turbine shaft drives compressor
Compressor pressurises intake air
Higher density air = more combustion power
Uses waste exhaust energy
Minimal parasitic power loss
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the differences between aviation gasoline (AVGAS) and motor gasoline (MOGAS).
concept: AVGAS (aviation gasoline, typically AVGAS 100LL for training and light aircraft) is specifically formulated for aircraft engines. It contains lead as an anti-knock additive (tetraethyl lead), which boosts octane rating. The 'LL' means 'low-lead'—newer formulation with reduced lead (about 0.56 grams per litre) compared to older formulations. AVGAS is manufactured to strict aviation standards (ASTM D910), with controlled volatility, purity, and octane. Motor gasoline (MOGAS) is fuel for cars—designed for automotive engines with different characteristics. MOGAS has much higher volatility (more prone to vapour lock in aircraft at altitude), typically lower octane (premium MOGAS might be 95 octane vs AVGAS 100), and contains ethanol (typically 5-10%) which can corrode fuel system components and separate from water in tanks. MOGAS's cheaper, but it's not approved for most aircraft engines unless specifically authorised by the manufacturer. Using MOGAS in an aircraft engine that's not approved for it can cause detonation, fouling, and fuel system damage. Always use the fuel grade specified in your aircraft's POH.
exam_tip: AVGAS: leads-containing (except unleaded variants), strict aviation spec, 100LL standard. MOGAS: automotive, cheaper, ethanol, not approved for most aircraft. Know the differences and why AVGAS is required.
key_facts: AVGAS 100LL: tetraethyl lead anti-knock, low-lead formula
ASTM D910 spec, controlled volatility
MOGAS: automotive fuel, higher volatility
MOGAS contains ethanol (5-10%)
MOGAS lower octane and cheaper
MOGAS not approved unless specified in POH
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Explain fuel octane ratings and performance numbers.
concept: Octane rating measures a fuel's resistance to detonation. The scale is based on two reference fuels: iso-octane (which resists detonation well, rated 100) and n-heptane (which detonates easily, rated 0). A fuel with a 100 octane rating behaves like iso-octane. In simple terms, higher octane = higher detonation resistance. AVGAS 100 means the fuel matches 100 octane iso-octane in anti-knock performance. Some high-octane fuels use a performance number system (PN), especially older military specifications, where the number can exceed 100 (like 115/145 PN). The first number is the lean mixture rating; the second is the rich mixture rating. Higher PN means better knock resistance, allowing higher power settings or compression ratios. Your aircraft's POH specifies the minimum octane required—use that grade or higher. Using lower octane risks detonation; using excessively higher octane (whilst not harmful) is wasteful. At places like Ardmore, you'll always use AVGAS 100LL unless your aircraft's approved for something different.
exam_tip: Octane rating = detonation resistance. 100 = iso-octane reference. Higher is better for high-compression engines. Know minimum spec for your aircraft.
key_facts: Octane scale based on iso-octane (100) and n-heptane (0)
Higher octane = more detonation resistance
Performance number: PN-115/145 format
Use minimum octane specified in POH
Higher octane than required is wasteful, not harmful
AVGAS 100LL standard for most training aircraft
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Explain the likely result of using a higher grade, or a lower grade, of fuel than that recommended for a given aircraft.
concept: Using higher octane than recommended (e.g., 130/135 in an engine spec'd for 100/130): generally harmless. The engine will run slightly cooler because higher-octane fuel burns more slowly, which is fine. You might see marginally better performance at very high power or altitude, but it's minimal. The downside is cost—premium fuel's expensive, and you're wasting money for negligible benefit. Using lower octane than recommended (e.g., 80/87 in an engine spec'd for 100/130): dangerous. The fuel's more prone to detonation, and at the power settings your engine's designed for, it will knock. Sustained detonation damages pistons, valves, bearings, and rings. You lose power (knock forces you to reduce power), and the engine develops problems. Progressive damage from chronic low-octane usage eventually causes complete failure. If you're at Hamilton and only 80-octane's available, your only option is to operate at reduced power (climb more slowly, etc.) to stay below the detonation threshold. Better to not fly than to risk detonation damage.
exam_tip: Higher octane: safe, unnecessary cost. Lower octane: detonation risk, engine damage. Always use specified grade.
key_facts: Higher octane: safe, marginal benefit, unnecessary cost
Lower octane: detonation risk, progressive damage
Use minimum octane specified in POH
Reduced power operation if lower octane only available
Don't fly if proper fuel unavailable
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the caution against using automobile fuel (MOGAS) in an aircraft engine, unless specifically authorised.
concept: MOGAS (automobile petrol) seems logical—it's cheaper and available everywhere. But there are critical issues. First, ethanol content (typically 5-10% in NZ pump fuel) causes corrosion in some aircraft fuel system components (rubber, seals, coatings) and can separate from water in tanks, creating a water layer that gums up fuel system. Second, volatility is too high—MOGAS boils at lower temperatures, creating vapour lock in aircraft fuel systems at altitude (fuel boils to vapour, blocks lines). Third, AVGAS is formulated for aviation use with strict quality controls; MOGAS is automotive and subject to different (less stringent) specs. Fourth, most aircraft engines are approved only for AVGAS; the fuel system and engine aren't validated for MOGAS. Insurance becomes invalid if you use unauthorised fuel and damage results. Some experimental or light-sport aircraft are specifically approved for MOGAS, and there's a whole STCed system for certain models, but that requires STC and POH approval. Unless your aircraft specifically says 'MOGAS approved' in writing, use AVGAS. The cost saving isn't worth the risk to the engine and systems.
exam_tip: MOGAS tempting but dangerous: ethanol corrosion, vapour lock, fuel system damage, engine damage. Caution: don't use unless explicitly approved in POH and STC.
key_facts: MOGAS contains ethanol (corrosion risk)
Higher volatility (vapour lock at altitude)
Not validated for aircraft engines
Insurance invalid if unauthorised fuel used
Some aircraft STC'd for MOGAS (rare)
Use AVGAS unless POH specifically allows MOGAS
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the distinguishing features of aviation turbine fuel (AVTUR/Jet A1).
concept: Aviation turbine fuel (Jet A1, also called AVTUR in some regions) is used in gas turbine engines (jets). It's a kerosene-like fuel, quite different from AVGAS. Jet A1 is less volatile than AVGAS—it boils at higher temperatures, so vapour lock isn't a concern. It's thicker, more like diesel, and flows more slowly. Colour is typically clear to pale straw (amber-ish), and it has a distinctive odour. Flash point is around 38-66°C depending on specification. It requires heating systems in the aircraft to prevent gelling at high altitude (very cold temperatures up there). Jet A1 contains no lead additives. You'll see it at major airports and training facilities with turbine aircraft. Never, ever use Jet fuel in a piston engine—it won't ignite properly in a piston engine's spark-plug ignition system, and it'll gum up everything. Piston engines absolutely require AVGAS.
exam_tip: Jet A1: turbine fuel, kerosene-like, no lead, less volatile, thicker. Different from AVGAS. Never mix them up.
key_facts: Jet A1: for gas turbine engines only
Kerosene-like consistency
Clear to pale straw colour
Flash point 38-66°C
No lead additives
Gels at very cold temps (requires heating)
Never use in piston engines
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: State the difference between the decals used on AVTUR and AVGAS fuelling equipment.
concept: Aviation facilities use colour-coded decals and labels on fuel equipment to prevent mixing fuels (which would be catastrophic). AVGAS fuelling equipment is marked with red decals/labels with white lettering—typically saying 'AVGAS' and listing the octane (100LL, 80/87, etc.). Jet fuel (AVTUR/Jet A1) equipment is marked with black decals/labels. This colour distinction makes it immediately obvious which fuel type you're accessing—red for petrol, black for jet. Some airports also use different hose connectors (AVGAS uses a certain size, Jet uses another) as an extra mechanical safety measure. Never ignore these labels. Using wrong fuel destroys engines and creates safety hazards. At any airport, the fuel colour and labels are your first check—always verify before opening the filler cap.
exam_tip: AVGAS equipment: red decals. Jet fuel equipment: black decals. Verify before fuelling. Never ignore colour coding.
key_facts: AVGAS: red decals/labels
Jet fuel: black decals/labels
Colour-coding prevents accidental mixing
Different hose connectors also prevent mix-up
Always verify colour before fuelling
Using wrong fuel = engine failure
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Explain the precautions which can be taken to avoid fuel contamination with water and other impurities.
concept: Fuel contamination's a serious issue. Water can separate in fuel tanks (especially in humid climates like New Zealand's coasts), and if it reaches the engine, it stops combustion and causes corrosion. Dirt, dust, and microbial growth are other contaminants. Precautions: always use a fuel quality check before flight—sump the tanks (drain a small amount into a clear container) and visually inspect for water (appears as separate layer or cloudiness), dirt, or discolouration. Store aircraft with fuel caps tight and filler openings sealed to prevent rain ingress. Keep fuel supplier containers sealed. When fuelling, use filtered nozzles and never leave tanks open unattended. Regularly drain fuel sumps to remove accumulated water and sediment. At busy training bases like Ardmore, fuel trucks should be regularly tested for contamination. In humid conditions, use fuel additive (biocide) occasionally to prevent microbial growth. Never use fuel from unknown sources. And never trust 'it looks okay'—always sump and inspect.
exam_tip: Precautions: sump before flight, sealed storage, filtered nozzles, sealed caps, regular sump drains, avoid unknown fuel sources.
key_facts: Sump tanks before every flight
Check for water, dirt, discolouration
Sealed storage and caps prevent water ingress
Filtered nozzles during fuelling
Regular sump drains remove contamination
Biocide additive prevents microbial growth
Never use unknown fuel sources
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Explain the special precautions which must be taken when fuelling from drum stock.
concept: Fuelling from drums (stored fuel containers) at remote strips or when main supply's unavailable requires extra care. Drums can have accumulated water and sediment; they're not quality-controlled like fuel trucks. Always inspect the drum exterior first—don't use rusty, damaged, or questionable drums. Before opening, ensure the drum's been stationary for several hours so sediment settles. Use a clean, sealed funnel with a fine mesh strainer when pouring fuel into the aircraft. Pour slowly and steadily to avoid splashing and contamination. Visually inspect the fuel as it flows—any cloudiness, water separation, or particles means stop immediately. Never fill aircraft to absolute maximum when using drums; leave some headspace to prevent overflow if temperature changes. Keep the area clean (no dust or dirt blowing around). After fuelling, immediately cap and seal the aircraft filler. And critically: sump the tanks before flight to catch any contamination that made it through. Drums are last resort; always prefer fuel trucks when available.
exam_tip: Drum fuelling: inspect drum, let sediment settle, use clean strainer, pour slowly, visually check fuel, sump before flight.
key_facts: Inspect drums for rust/damage
Let sediment settle before opening
Use fine-mesh strainer funnel
Pour slowly and watch for contamination
Visually inspect fuel colour/clarity
Never fill to absolute maximum
Sump tanks before flight
Prefer fuel trucks when available
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Explain the reasons for the avoidance of non-approved plastic containers.
concept: Non-approved plastic containers can leach chemicals into fuel (especially in warm conditions or with certain fuel types) and degrade, causing particles to contaminate the fuel. Some plastics absorb water through their walls, allowing moisture to enter the fuel. Approved fuel containers (aviation-specific metal or approved plastic) are designed to resist these issues. AVGAS can dissolve some plastics, causing failure; Jet fuel similarly attacks incompatible plastics. Unauthorised containers might not seal properly, allowing air exposure and oxidation of fuel. They can also generate static electricity (non-conductive plastics) during transfer, creating fire risk. And they're often not certified for aviation fuel handling, so if there's damage and fuel spill or contamination, liability's yours. Always use approved containers—either metal aviation fuel cans or containers specifically rated for AVGAS/Jet A1. They're not expensive and they're essential for fuel integrity and safety.
exam_tip: Non-approved plastic: leaches chemicals, absorbs water, degrades, generates static. Use approved containers only.
key_facts: Non-approved plastic can leach chemicals
Absorbs moisture through walls
AVGAS and Jet fuel dissolve incompatible plastics
Static electricity generation fire risk
Poor sealing allows oxidation
Always use approved aviation fuel containers
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of the exhaust manifold.
concept: The exhaust manifold's a system of pipes that collects hot exhaust gases from individual cylinders and combines them into a single flow. Each cylinder has an exhaust port leading into the manifold. As each cylinder completes its power stroke, the exhaust valve opens and burnt gases are expelled into the manifold, which channels them toward the tail pipe (and eventually out the engine exhaust). The manifold's designed to accommodate the pressure pulses from each cylinder's exhaust stroke, minimizing back-pressure. Modern manifolds often have runners of different lengths to optimise scavenging (the removal of exhaust from one cylinder doesn't interfere with another). The manifold gets extremely hot—400-600°C—and in some aircraft, it's used as the heat source for carburettor heat. The manifold must be well-sealed; any leaks allow hot exhaust to escape into the engine compartment, creating fire risk and heat damage.
exam_tip: Manifold collects exhaust from cylinders, combines into single flow, very hot. Seal integrity critical for safety.
key_facts: Collects exhaust from all cylinders
Channels to tail pipe/engine exhaust
Extremely hot (400-600°C)
Used for carb heat source
Must be properly sealed
Back-pressure affects scavenging
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the importance of proper sealing of the exhaust manifold.
concept: The exhaust manifold operates at very high temperatures and pressure. If it leaks, hot gases escape into the engine compartment, creating several hazards. First, fire risk: 400-600°C exhaust in contact with fuel lines, hydraulic fluid, or electrical insulation causes fires. Second, asphyxiation risk: carbon monoxide (CO) can leak into the cabin if the manifold-to-cabin seal fails; CO's odourless and deadly. Third, corrosion: hot exhaust exposure degrades nearby components. Fourth, performance loss: any leak reduces back-pressure, which affects cylinder scavenging and power output. A cracked or loose manifold is a serious maintenance issue. You'd check for discolouration (heat damage) around manifold joints, listen for leaks during run-up, and look for any visible cracks during inspection. Poor seal integrity isn't something you patch; it requires repair or replacement. At pre-flight, if you notice discolouration or smell anything weird (burnt metal, unidentified odours), investigate the manifold.
exam_tip: Proper sealing prevents: fire, CO ingestion, corrosion, performance loss. Cracked manifold is maintenance item, not something to ignore.
key_facts: Prevents hot exhaust escape and fire
Prevents carbon monoxide ingress
Preserves system back-pressure
Protects nearby components from heat
Cracks require repair/replacement
Not a field-fix item
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the possible sources, indications and associated danger of carbon monoxide gas.
concept: Carbon monoxide (CO) is a colourless, odourless poisonous gas produced by incomplete combustion in the engine. Sources: exhaust manifold cracks or loose joints allowing CO into the cabin, defective cabin heater systems that route manifold heat into the cabin but allow gas mixing, or propane heaters in closed cabins. Indications of CO poisoning in the cockpit: headache (often the first sign), dizziness, nausea, flu-like symptoms, and in severe cases, loss of consciousness. The insidious part: CO symptoms resemble hypoxia, so pilots sometimes respond incorrectly (thinking it's altitude when it's poisoning). CO at high altitude is particularly dangerous because hypoxia masks it. Dangers: complete unconsciousness and death if exposure continues. Prevention: ensure manifold's properly sealed (no leaks), cabin heaters properly maintained, and any propane heater properly vented outside. Some aircraft have CO detectors (colour-changing patches or electronic) which change colour if CO's present—these are helpful safety items. If you suspect CO poisoning in flight: open all windows, close cabin heat (shut off the source), descend, and land immediately. Never ignore symptoms that might be CO.
exam_tip: CO: colourless, odourless, from exhaust leaks. Symptoms: headache, dizziness, nausea. Response: open windows, close heat, land. Prevent with manifold sealing.
key_facts: CO from incomplete combustion
Leaks from manifold cracks/joints
Colourless and odourless
Symptoms: headache, dizziness, nausea
Resembles hypoxia symptoms
Detection: colour-changing detectors
Prevention: manifold sealing, heater maintenance
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Explain the reasons for fitting independent dual ignition systems to engines.
concept: Aircraft engines use two independent magnetos (not one big one) for several critical reasons. First, redundancy: if one magneto fails, the other continues firing the plugs, so you've still got partial power and can keep flying (though degraded). Second, safety: losing ignition means losing the engine; dual ignition significantly reduces that risk. Third, reliability: magnetos are mechanical and wear; two independent systems spread the risk. Fourth, better engine scavenging and power: each magneto fires its own set of spark plugs, so you get two sparks per power stroke (one from each magneto), creating a broader burn and more complete combustion. This improves power output and efficiency. Fifth, improved starting: with two magnetos firing, starting's easier. The trade-off: complexity and cost, but the reliability and safety benefits are worth it. Every piston trainer you fly has dual mags. The ignition switch typically has positions: off, left mag only, right mag only, and both. During magneto checks before flight, you test each mag independently to ensure both are functioning.
exam_tip: Dual mags: redundancy if one fails, better combustion (two sparks), improved starting. Why aircraft don't use single large magneto.
key_facts: Redundancy: one fails, one continues
Better combustion from dual sparks
Improved starting capability
Increases safety and reliability
Each mag fires its own plugs
Tested independently during run-up
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: List the essential components of an ignition system.
concept: An ignition system starts with the magneto (generates electrical power and timing). The magneto produces high-tension (HT) output that goes to a distributor (routes the spark to the right cylinder at the right time). From there, ignition harness (high-tension leads, aka spark plug wires) carries the spark to each spark plug. The spark plugs (in each cylinder) create the actual spark gap where ignition occurs. An impulse coupling (mechanical device) on the magneto ensures a hot spark during starting by retarding spark timing momentarily. Some systems also include a starting vibrator that helps establish initial oscillation. Capacitors in the magneto and wiring help sharpen the spark. The magneto itself contains a rotating magnet, coils, and electrical components that generate and time the spark. All these components work together; failure of any single component means loss of ignition and loss of that magneto (hence the need for dual systems).
exam_tip: Magneto, distributor, ignition harness, spark plugs, impulse coupling. Know each component's role.
key_facts: Magneto: power generation and timing
Distributor: routes spark to cylinders
Ignition harness: spark plug wires
Spark plugs: create ignition spark
Impulse coupling: aids starting
Capacitors: sharpen spark
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the operation of the following: (a) an aircraft magneto; (b) the distributor; (c) ignition harness (high tension leads); (d) spark plugs; (e) impulse couplings.
concept: Magneto: A rotating magnet (rotor) inside the magneto generates magnetic field. This rotating field induces voltage in stationary coils (pickup coils) inside the magneto. The voltage is low initially, then passes through a capacitor circuit that builds voltage, creating a high-voltage pulse (thousands of volts). This pulse is timed to occur just before each cylinder's top dead centre. Distributor: Rotates in sync with the engine and has contact points for each cylinder. As the distributor rotor spins, it sequentially connects the magneto's high-voltage output to the correct spark plug wires in firing order. Ignition harness (spark plug wires): These are high-voltage cables connecting the distributor output to each spark plug. They're shielded to prevent electrical interference and are designed to withstand thousands of volts. Spark plugs: Inside each cylinder, two electrodes (centre and ground) are separated by a small gap. The high-voltage pulse jumps the gap, creating the spark that ignites the mixture. The gap spacing's critical—too small and spark's weak, too large and won't fire. Impulse coupling: A mechanical device on the magneto that temporarily retards spark timing during engine start (slow crank). Once the engine fires and speeds up, the impulse coupling disengages and normal advance applies. This ensures a hot spark even at low crank speeds.
exam_tip: Magneto generates high-voltage pulse. Distributor routes to correct plug. Wires carry pulse. Plugs create spark gap. Impulse coupling aids starting.
key_facts: Magneto: rotating magnet induces voltage
Capacitor circuit boosts to high voltage
Distributor: sequentially routes to each plug
Ignition harness: shielded HV cables
Spark plugs: gap where spark jumps
Impulse coupling: retards timing for starting
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the purpose of fitting an impulse coupling to a magneto.
concept: During engine start, the starter motor cranks the engine very slowly (perhaps 50-100 RPM). At that speed, the magneto's rotating magnet spins slowly, generating very weak voltage—barely enough to fire the spark plugs reliably. You'd get a weak, late spark, making starting difficult or impossible. An impulse coupling solves this by mechanically retarding spark timing during start. As the engine cranks slowly, the impulse coupling holds back the magneto's rotor (delays its rotation slightly), allowing magnetic field to build up more before the spark fires. When the spark finally does occur, it's much hotter and more reliable. Once the engine fires and speed increases above about 1000 RPM, centrifugal weights inside the impulse coupling overcome the retard mechanism, and the coupling disengages. Normal spark advance then applies automatically. So the impulse coupling's purpose: ensure hot, reliable spark during the slow cranking of engine start, then get out of the way once the engine's running. Without it, cold starting would be much harder.
exam_tip: Impulse coupling retards timing during slow crank, ensuring hot spark for starting. Disengages once engine speeds up. Why starting's reliable.
key_facts: Retards spark timing during start
Allows field buildup for hotter spark
Engages at slow crank speed
Disengages around 1000 RPM
Ensures reliable cold start
Automatically managed, pilot doesn't control it
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the reason ignition is timed to occur before top dead centre (TDC).
concept: Combustion doesn't happen instantaneously. When the spark plug fires, it ignites the fuel-air mixture, but the flame front takes several milliseconds to propagate through the combustion chamber. If you waited until the piston reached top dead centre (TDC) to fire the spark, by the time combustion developed, the piston would already be starting downward, and you'd waste the combustion energy. Instead, spark fires before TDC (typically 20-30 degrees before), so by the time the piston reaches TDC and starts its power stroke downward, combustion is fully developed and creates maximum pressure right when you need it—pushing the piston hard from the very start of the downstroke. This is called 'spark advance.' The amount of advance varies with engine speed and operating conditions; faster engines need more advance (longer lead time for combustion), slower engines need less. Modern high-altitude engines might have 35+ degrees of advance at high RPM. Getting spark timing right is critical for efficiency and power.
exam_tip: Spark before TDC because combustion takes time. Peak pressure needed right at start of power stroke = maximum force = most power.
key_facts: Spark before TDC = spark advance
Allows time for combustion development
Peak pressure at right moment = max power
Advance increases with engine speed
Typical 20-35 degrees before TDC
Critical for efficiency
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the operation of the ignition switch(es) in the cockpit.
concept: The ignition switch is typically a 4-position rotary switch (sometimes 5 on complex aircraft): OFF, LEFT MAG, RIGHT MAG, and BOTH. OFF: both magnetos are grounded and disabled; no ignition. LEFT MAG: left magneto only is active; right magneto is grounded. RIGHT MAG: right magneto only is active; left magneto is grounded. BOTH: both magnetos active—normal flight operation. (Some aircraft have a START position where the magnetos and starter motor are energised together.) During start, you'd typically place the switch in BOTH, and the starter motor cranks the engine while both magnetos provide spark. Once running, you stay in BOTH. During magneto checks, you momentarily switch to LEFT or RIGHT individually to test each one independently. The spark plug wires and distributor are wired so that each magneto's spark always goes to the correct plugs; the switch just controls power to each magneto. A failed magneto or switch would leave one magneto active (the other grounded), giving you asymmetrical ignition—rough running but still flight-capable.
exam_tip: OFF, LEFT, RIGHT, BOTH positions. BOTH for normal flight. LEFT/RIGHT for testing. OFF for security. Know the switch function.
key_facts: OFF: both grounded, no ignition
LEFT: left mag active, right grounded
RIGHT: right mag active, left grounded
BOTH: both active (normal flight)
START: adds starter motor (some aircraft)
Used for mag checking and starting
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Outline the correct procedures to be followed during magneto checks.
concept: Before every flight, you perform magneto checks during the run-up to ensure both magnetos are functioning correctly. Procedure: first, run the engine at a safe RPM (typically 1000-1500 RPM, as specified in the POH). Ensure the aircraft is in a stable configuration (trim set, flight surfaces free). With the engine running smoothly at BOTH, note the RPM. Slowly move the ignition switch to LEFT MAG and note the RPM drop. The drop should be modest—typically 50-150 RPM depending on the aircraft. Check that the drop is smooth and the engine doesn't surge or miss badly. Return to BOTH and wait for the engine to stabilise. Repeat with RIGHT MAG. The right-side RPM drop might be slightly different from the left (that's normal, as magnetos aren't perfectly matched). Critical checks: the RPM drop on each side shouldn't exceed the aircraft's limits (typically 150 RPM, but check the POH). The difference between left and right drops shouldn't exceed about 50 RPM. If one mag is significantly worse than the other, or if there's rough running, surging, or backfiring, there's a problem—don't fly. Also check that returning to BOTH smoothly increases RPM back to the original reading. Never test magnetos with a passenger unfamiliar with the procedure; they might get alarmed at the RPM drop.
exam_tip: Mag checks: smooth RPM drop on each side (50-150 RPM typical). Right-left difference max ~50 RPM. No surging. Back to BOTH should restore RPM smoothly.
key_facts: Perform at 1000-1500 RPM stable setting
Test LEFT, then RIGHT individually
Note RPM drop on each side
Drop should be smooth and modest
Right-left difference max ~50 RPM
Return to BOTH should restore RPM
Rough or excessive drop = maintenance issue
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the indications of various problems manifested during a magneto check.
concept: During mag checks, you observe several potential problems. Excessive RPM drop (more than 150 RPM on either side, or asymmetric—right much worse than left): indicates magneto degradation, worn brushes, or carbon buildup. Rough running or missing when on one mag: spark plugs might be fouled, or the magneto's misfiring. Surging (RPM rises and falls irregularly): might indicate a bad capacitor or intermittent contact in the magneto. Backfiring (popping sound from the exhaust): timing issue or magneto delivering spark at wrong time. Inability to return to smooth operation after mag check: again suggests magneto or plug issues. A dead mag (no RPM drop on one side, or massive drop close to shut-down): magneto's completely failed. Any of these warrant maintenance investigation. Some issues (fouled spark plugs from over-rich running) can be cleared by leaning and running at higher power for a bit; others (internal magneto failure) require replacement. Never ignore mag check problems—they indicate a safety issue that needs addressing before flight.
exam_tip: Excessive drop, asymmetry, rough, surge, backfire, dead mag = problems. Know which are critical (mag failure) vs potentially clearable (fouled plugs).
key_facts: Excessive RPM drop: magneto wear
Asymmetry: one mag worse than other
Rough/missing: fouled plugs or misfire
Surging: capacitor or contact issues
Backfiring: timing problem
Dead mag: complete failure
Some clearable, some need maintenance
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Explain the causes of spark plug fouling and the methods of clearing such fouling.
concept: Spark plug fouling happens when the spark gap becomes blocked by deposits, preventing the spark from jumping. Causes: running too rich (excess fuel creates carbon deposits and wets the plug), operating at low power for extended periods (plug doesn't get hot enough to self-clean), cold-starting (rich mixture to aid starting leaves deposits), or aged spark plugs (gap widens, deposits accumulate). Fouled plugs create a no-spark condition and misfire, rough running, and power loss. Clearing methods: lean the mixture (reduce fuel flow to burn off deposits and let plugs heat up), increase power (climb to cruise power where plugs self-clean—the hotter the operating temperature, the better plugs clean), or rough-running procedures (some POHs specify running at higher RPM to blow out fouling). If fouling persists, the plugs need actual cleaning or replacement by maintenance. At Hamilton or Christchurch, if you start cold and run too rich too long, you might foul plugs before even taking off; the fix is to lean and run at higher power to self-clean. Regular proper mixture management prevents fouling.
exam_tip: Over-rich = fouling. Fix: lean mixture, climb to cruise power for self-cleaning. Know why proper mixture management's important.
key_facts: Over-rich running = primary cause
Low-power operation = deposits accumulate
Fouling blocks spark gap
Symptoms: misfire, rough, power loss
Fix: lean mixture, increase power
Plugs self-clean at higher operating temp
Persistent fouling needs maintenance
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the components of a typical solid state ignition system.
concept: Solid state ignition replaces magnetos and mechanical contact points with electronic components. Key parts: electronic control unit (ECU, a small computer), power supply (battery or alternator), sensor(s) to detect engine speed and piston position (usually a magnetic pickup near the crankshaft), capacitor discharge module (stores and releases high voltage), ignition coil(s), and spark plugs. Some systems have dual ECUs and dual coils for redundancy (like dual mags). The ECU calculates the ideal spark timing based on engine speed, manifold pressure, and other parameters, then signals the coil to fire at exactly the right moment. No mechanical timing, no moving parts in the ignition circuit—just electrons. Because of this, reliability's very high (no brushes to wear, no contacts to pit). The system's dependent on electrical power; if battery or alternator fails, ignition fails (unlike magnetos, which generate their own power). Some aircraft have backup power systems to protect against total electrical failure.
exam_tip: Solid state: ECU, sensors, coil, no mechanical parts. Reliable, dependent on electrics. Know components and why it's different from magnetos.
key_facts: Electronic control unit (ECU)
Engine speed/position sensors
Power supply (battery/alternator)
Capacitor discharge module
Ignition coil(s)
Spark plugs
No mechanical timing components
Highly reliable if power available
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the operation of a typical solid state ignition system.
concept: Operation is elegant in its simplicity from the pilot's perspective. The sensor (magnetic pickup) detects crankshaft rotation and sends signals to the ECU. The ECU processes these signals and any other inputs (manifold pressure, engine temperature, etc.) to calculate ideal spark timing. It then sends an electrical command to the coil(s), which builds up voltage and releases it (capacitor discharge) to the spark plugs at precisely the right moment. The timing's automatically optimised for current conditions—high power gets more advance, low power less advance, etc. From the flight deck, you don't think about spark timing; you just fly. The system handles it. Diagnostics: some systems have built-in self-tests that run when you turn on the master switch; annunciators or LEDs indicate system health. Failure modes are typically binary: system works or it doesn't (unlike magnetos, where degradation is gradual). If the ECU senses a sensor failure or other problem, it might go into 'limp home' mode where it defaults to a safe basic timing for limited operation.
exam_tip: Sensor → ECU → calculates timing → signals coil → spark. Automatic, optimised. Pilot doesn't manually adjust spark timing.
key_facts: Sensor signals ECU of crankshaft position
ECU calculates ideal timing
Commands coil to fire at right moment
Timing automatically optimised for conditions
No pilot adjustment of spark timing
Self-test capability
Usually binary: works or doesn't
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the purpose for conducting typical solid state ignition integrity checks.
concept: Solid state systems include built-in self-diagnostics. Integrity checks verify that the system's functioning: all components are communicating, the sensors are detecting properly, the coil's charging, and the spark plugs are firing. These checks catch failures before flight. Purpose: ensure the ignition system's operational and safe. Unlike magneto checks (where you're listening to the engine's response), solid state checks often involve a test switch or automatic self-test that runs when you activate master switch. The system's either green-light good or flashing a fault code. This reduces ambiguity (no interpretation needed like with mag checks) and catches problems earlier. If a check fails, you don't fly until maintenance resolves it.
exam_tip: Purpose: verify system's operational before flight. Catches failures early. Automatic or test-switch based.
key_facts: Built-in self-test capability
Verifies all components communication
Detects sensor, coil, plug issues
Usually automatic on master activation
Clear pass/fail indication
Prevents flying with degraded system
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the procedure for conducting typical solid state ignition integrity checks.
concept: Exact procedures vary by system, so always consult the POH. General procedure: activate master switch; the system self-test runs automatically (usually a few seconds). LED indicators or annunciators show result—green/OK light means system's healthy; red/fault light means problem. Some systems allow manual test by pressing a test button; you'd hold it briefly and watch for the correct response (spark at plugs, coil responding, etc.). On more basic systems, you'd simply observe that the system powers up correctly when master's on and instruments show normal readings. Some aircraft also have a ground-check procedure: with master on and engine off, you can verify certain system functions without running. Once you start the engine, the system's continuously self-monitoring during flight. If a problem develops, an annunciator or warning light alerts you. Never skip these checks; solid state systems depend on power and electronics, so verifying they're healthy before flight is essential.
exam_tip: Check POH for exact procedure. Usually: master on, observe self-test, verify pass. No major interpretation like mag checks.
key_facts: Master on activates self-test
Observe LED/annunciator indication
Manual test button on some systems
Green = OK, red/fault = problem
System continuously monitors in flight
Follow POH exactly
Don't fly if test fails
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: With reference to a solid state ignition system: (a) explain the significance of maintaining the ignition power supply; (b) describe a typical power supply backup system.
concept: Significance of power supply: solid state ignition is entirely electrical; no power, no spark. Unlike magnetos (which generate their own power), solid state depends on battery and alternator. If alternator fails in flight, the battery supplies power, but only for a limited time. If battery was already low or the alternator failed due to some serious electrical fault, you could lose all electrical power and therefore ignition. Power loss = engine failure. So maintaining the ignition power supply is critical: keep your alternator healthy (check outputs and loads regularly), maintain the battery, and ensure electrical connections are clean and tight. A typical backup system: many advanced aircraft have a standby power supply (basically a separate battery) that automatically engages if the main alternator fails. It supplies essential systems including ignition until you can land. Some systems use a capacitor network instead of a backup battery—it won't last long (minutes), but it's enough to fly the aircraft to an airport. Without any backup, you're vulnerable to total electrical failure. That's why many operators prefer magnetos in remote areas; they're independent of electrics.
exam_tip: Power supply critical for solid state. Alternator failure = risk of ignition loss. Backup systems provide time to land safely.
key_facts: No power = no spark (entirely electrical)
Depends on battery and alternator
Alternator failure starts battery drain
Limited time on battery alone
Backup power supply extends time
Standby battery or capacitor common
Maintenance of alternator/battery critical
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, AIP NZ

syllabus_desc: Describe the functioning of the starter motor.
concept: A starter motor's an electric motor powered by the aircraft battery. When you engage the starter (via a cockpit switch), battery current flows through a solenoid (electromagnet relay). The solenoid pulls a mechanical linkage that engages a gear on the starter shaft with a larger gear on the engine's flywheel. Simultaneously, the solenoid completes a heavy-current circuit that powers the motor itself. The motor's armature (rotating coil) spins in a magnetic field, creating torque. This torque, multiplied by the gear ratio (small gear on starter, large gear on flywheel), provides enough force to crank the engine over against compression resistance. Once the engine fires and speed exceeds starter speed, a one-way clutch (sprag clutch) in the starter gear disengages—the engine stops driving the starter back. Current ceases (you've released the starter switch), and the solenoid releases, disengaging the gear. Simple but effective.
exam_tip: Solenoid engages gear and powers motor. Motor drives flywheel gear. One-way clutch releases after engine start.
key_facts: Battery powers starter motor via solenoid
Solenoid engages starter gear to flywheel
Electric motor creates cranking torque
Gear reduction multiplies torque
One-way clutch disengages after start
Solenoid releases when starter switch released
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the operational limitations of the starter motor.
concept: Starter motors draw enormous current—several hundred amps—from the battery. This is only sustainable briefly; prolonged cranking drains the battery quickly. Typical cranking duration: 5-10 seconds maximum. If the engine doesn't catch in that time, you release the starter, wait 30 seconds to let the battery recover, then try again. Multiple rapid starts in succession can completely drain the battery. Starter motors also generate intense heat during operation. Continuous operation (beyond 10 seconds) can overheat the armature and damage windings. POH specifies maximum continuous crank time (often 10 seconds) and mandatory cool-down periods between attempts. Starter motors are designed for intermittent duty, not continuous running. In very cold conditions or with a weak battery, starting becomes difficult or impossible—the motor spins slowly, generating weak cranking torque. At places like Christchurch in winter, you might need GPU (ground power unit) assistance for reliable starting. Maintenance: if the starter turns slowly or won't engage (solenoid clicks but motor doesn't crank), the battery or electrical connections are likely faulty.
exam_tip: Starter draws huge current, brief use only. Max 10 seconds cranking, then cool-down. Multiple starts risk battery depletion.
key_facts: Draws 300+ amps from battery
Generates intense heat
Intermittent duty only
Max 10 seconds continuous cranking
Requires 30+ second cool-down between attempts
Multiple starts risk total discharge
Cold/weak battery complicates starting
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the correct operation of a typical starter switch.
concept: A typical starter switch is a spring-loaded momentary switch (not a maintained contact). You push it to engage the starter; release it, and it springs back to neutral (off) automatically. The switch position: OFF (nothing), and momentary CRANK (held by pushing). Some aircraft also have a START position that combines magnetos + starter, but the principle's the same—spring-loaded. Correct operation: ensure master switch is on and the magnetos are in BOTH. Push the starter switch and hold it while the engine cranks. Listen and feel for the engine to fire. The moment it fires consistently (first few cylinders catching), release the starter switch. If the engine doesn't catch after 10 seconds, release the switch, wait 30 seconds (POH might specify longer), then try again. Never hold the starter engaged after the engine's running—you're just wasting battery and overheating the motor. Some older aircraft have manual controls where you have to actively release the starter; modern ones use spring-loaded switches that disengage automatically. The golden rule: brief cranking, immediate release once running.
exam_tip: Push and hold starter switch while engine cranks. Release as soon as engine fires. Never leave starter engaged with running engine.
key_facts: Spring-loaded momentary switch (push to crank)
Releases automatically or manually
Master and magnetos on before starting
Hold through first ignition signs
Release immediately when engine catches
Wait 30+ seconds between attempts
Never leave starter engaged with running engine
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of the terms; (a) force; (b) work; (c) power; (d) energy; (e) torque.
concept: Force is a push or pull; measured in Newtons (N) or pounds (lb). Work is force applied over distance; it's measured in Joules (J) or foot-pounds (ft-lb). Power is the rate at which work is done—how much work per unit time; measured in watts (W) or horsepower (hp). Energy is the capacity to do work; same units as work. Torque is a twisting force—applied at a distance from a pivot point. Think of tightening a bolt with a wrench; the force you apply multiplied by the wrench length is torque (Newton-metres or foot-pounds). For engines: the piston's combustion force (force) pushes the crankshaft through an arc (work). The engine's rotating speed determines how fast this happens (power). A strong engine produces both high force (torque) and high power because of speed. Understanding these relationships helps explain why an engine's performance is defined by both torque and power—high torque at low RPM gives slow, strong output; high RPM at lower torque gives fast output.
exam_tip: Force = push/pull. Work = force × distance. Power = work / time. Torque = force × distance (rotational). Energy = capacity to work.
key_facts: Force measured in Newtons or pounds
Work: force applied over distance
Power: work per unit time (watts/hp)
Energy: capacity to do work
Torque: twisting force at distance
Combustion force = work. Repeated = power
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the relationship between horsepower and kilowatts.
concept: Horsepower and kilowatts are both units of power; they're just different scales. One horsepower is defined as 746 watts. So 1 hp = 0.746 kW, and conversely, 1 kW ≈ 1.34 hp. Modern specifications often list engine power in kilowatts for international standardisation, but traditional aircraft specs (especially in older documents) use horsepower. A typical light training twin might be rated at 160 hp per engine = about 119 kW per engine. A 100 hp trainer = about 75 kW. When you see a spec, just mentally convert if needed. Most exam questions will specify which unit they're using.
exam_tip: 1 hp = 746 watts = 0.746 kW. Multiply hp by 0.746 to get kW. Multiply kW by 1.34 to get hp.
key_facts: 1 hp = 746 watts
1 kW ≈ 1.34 hp
Both units express power (rate of work)
Know conversion for international specs
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of: (a) brake power; (b) rated power; (c) rated altitude (critical altitude); (d) full throttle height.
concept: Brake power is the actual power delivered by the engine shaft—the useful power you can extract and use (driving the propeller, etc.). It's measured by applying a controlled load (brake) to the shaft and measuring the torque and RPM needed to resist it. Rated power is the maximum continuous power the engine's approved to produce without damage; exceeding it shortens engine life. Most engines have both a takeoff power (high for 5 minutes) and a cruise power rating (sustainable indefinitely). Rated altitude (or critical altitude) is the altitude at which the engine can no longer produce rated power, even at full throttle. Above this altitude, power drops because air density's too low. A naturally-aspirated engine might have critical altitude of 8000-10,000 feet. Full throttle height is similar—it's the altitude where the engine reaches its rated power at full throttle (below this, it reaches rated power before hitting full throttle; above, it can't reach rated power even throttle-firewalled). For training, these matter because you know your engine's limitations—in the heat at Ardmore, you might not achieve rated power at sea level due to temperature. At altitude climbing out from Christchurch, power decreases as you climb.
exam_tip: Brake power = shaft power (what's available). Rated = max continuous approved. Critical altitude = loses rated power. Full throttle height = reaches rated power.
key_facts: Brake power: useful shaft power output
Rated power: maximum continuous approved
Takeoff vs cruise ratings differ
Critical altitude: power falls below rated
Full throttle height: reaches rated at full throttle
Power decreases above critical altitude
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the meaning of: (a) thermal efficiency; (b) mechanical efficiency; (c) volumetric efficiency.
concept: Thermal efficiency is how well the engine converts fuel's chemical energy into useful mechanical work. A perfect engine would convert 100% of fuel energy to work; reality's maybe 30-35% for a piston engine (the rest is lost as heat in exhaust, cooling system, friction, etc.). Modern engines with high compression ratios achieve better thermal efficiency than older, low-compression designs. Mechanical efficiency is how much of the power generated by combustion actually makes it to the crankshaft output (as opposed to being lost to internal friction, bearing drag, etc.). A well-tuned engine might lose 10-20% of combustion power to internal friction. Volumetric efficiency is the ratio of actual air mass drawn into the cylinder versus the theoretical maximum air mass at atmospheric pressure and temperature. A naturally-aspirated engine at sea level and low RPM achieves close to 100% volumetric efficiency. At altitude, with thinner air, it drops dramatically (maybe 60-70%). A supercharged or turbocharged engine can exceed 100% because the compressor forces more air than would normally flow in. These three efficiencies together define overall engine performance and power output.
exam_tip: Thermal = fuel to work conversion (~30%). Mechanical = friction losses (~10-20%). Volumetric = air mass actually drawn (100% at sea level, lower at altitude).
key_facts: Thermal efficiency: fuel to work conversion
Mechanical efficiency: friction losses
Volumetric efficiency: actual vs theoretical air mass
Thermal typically 30-35%
Mechanical 80-90%
Volumetric drops with altitude
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the relationship between typical brake power and power available curves.
concept: A brake power curve plots engine power (vertical axis) against RPM (horizontal axis). At low RPM, power's low. As RPM increases, power increases (more combustion cycles per minute). The curve typically rises steeply initially, then levels off, reaching a peak somewhere around cruise RPM, then might drop slightly or plateau at maximum RPM. A power-available curve (often used in aircraft performance analysis) shows how much power the propeller can absorb at various airspeeds and altitudes. At low airspeed, the propeller demands little power; as airspeed increases (denser air loading the propeller), power required increases dramatically. The intersection of available power (what the engine delivers) and power required (what the propeller needs at that airspeed) determines performance. Below critical altitude, maximum brake power's available at wide-open throttle. Above critical altitude, power drops despite full throttle. In level flight, the aircraft climbs when power available exceeds power required, descends when power required exceeds available, and flies level when they balance. Understanding both curves helps predict aircraft performance across the speed envelope and altitude.
exam_tip: Brake power curve: engine capability vs RPM. Power available: what propeller absorbs vs airspeed. Intersection determines performance balance.
key_facts: Brake power increases with RPM
Peaks near cruise RPM
Power available: propeller demand at each speed
Climb: available exceeds required
Level flight: balanced
Descent: required exceeds available
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the general conditions for the most efficient engine operation.
concept: Most efficient operation occurs at cruise power and altitude, where the engine's running at moderate settings (not maximum power, not low power), mixture's properly leaned, and ignition's optimally timed. Specific conditions: manifold pressure set to the value specified for cruise (typically 60-75% power), RPM set for the desired speed-efficiency trade-off (lower RPM = less fuel burn but slower; higher RPM = more speed but more fuel), mixture leaned to the smooth setting just before the power peak (around 17:1 to 18:1 ratio), engine temperature in the green band (not too hot, not too cold), and smooth flying (no excess maneuvring or attitude changes that waste energy). Avoid high-power climbs and descents that stress the engine and waste fuel. Smooth power transitions are more efficient than abrupt throttle changes. Flying at altitude (thinner air but cooler) often provides better efficiency than sea level (denser but hotter). Every engine's different; the POH specifies cruise power settings for best economy or best range. Following the POH exactly gives the best efficiency. Essentially: steady-state moderate-power flight with properly-leaned mixture.
exam_tip: Efficient operation: cruise power, altitude, leaned mixture, smooth flying. Avoid high power and abrupt changes.
key_facts: Cruise power (not max, not low)
Proper altitude (cooler, thinner air)
Mixture leaned to optimum
Smooth flying, minimal maneuvring
Engine temps in green band
POH-specified cruise settings
Steady-state operation
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of changing altitude on manifold pressure and power output of normally aspirated engines.
concept: A normally-aspirated engine (no supercharger or turbo) draws intake air directly from the atmosphere. As altitude increases, atmospheric pressure decreases, so air density decreases. The engine draws the same volume of air per stroke, but that volume contains fewer oxygen molecules. Manifold pressure (measured on the aircraft's manifold pressure gauge, which shows intake pressure) decreases with altitude. At sea level, manifold pressure might read 28-30 inches of mercury at full throttle. At 10,000 feet, it might read only 18 inches even with throttle wide open. Less air means less combustion force, so brake power decreases. The power decrease is roughly proportional to air density—at 10,000 feet (about 70% sea-level density), you'd have roughly 70% of sea-level power. This is why aircraft can't climb indefinitely; at some critical altitude, power available equals power required (no excess for further climb), and the aircraft reaches its service ceiling. Pilots manage this by descending power settings at altitude (e.g., 'at cruise, reduce to 65% power') to match what the atmosphere allows. Above critical altitude, the manifold pressure gauge becomes a power limiter rather than a throttle—you can't exceed the power available regardless of throttle position.
exam_tip: Higher altitude = lower air density = lower manifold pressure = lower power. Effect: power drop roughly matches density drop.
key_facts: Manifold pressure decreases with altitude
Air density decreases with altitude
Power output decreases proportionally
Roughly 70% power at 10,000 ft
Above critical altitude, power's density-limited
Climb performance worsens higher
Service ceiling: power available = power required
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the systems which typically require DC power in an aircraft.
concept: Your DC electrical system is the backbone of the aeroplane. Everything that needs power before you get the engines running—and quite a bit after—relies on that battery and alternator. Think about what happens when you're doing your walk-around at Ardmore or anywhere else: you need lights, you need the radio to talk to ATC, the fuel pump needs juice to get fuel to the engines. Once you're running, systems like landing lights, navigation lights, instrument panel lighting, the autopilot, and the entire avionics suite all depend on DC power. Even your flaps and landing gear on many twins use electrically-driven pumps or motors. The point is, without DC power, you're not flying very far.
exam_tip: List the major consumers: battery charging/starting, lighting (cockpit, nav, landing), radio, fuel pump, engine instruments, autopilot, landing gear/flaps, and avionics. Focus on what's essential for flight ops.
key_facts: DC power comes from the battery and alternator/generator
Critical systems: radio, fuel pump, engine instruments, lighting
Landing gear and flaps often use electric motors or pump drives
Autopilot and modern avionics require steady DC supply
Loss of electrical power means loss of radio comms and engine instrumentation
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the function(s) of the following in a typical electrical system: (a) the battery; (b) a ground power source; (c) the alternator or generator; (d) bus bars; (e) over voltage protection.
concept: Your battery is your electrical starter motor and emergency backup—it cranks the engine and provides power until the alternator takes over. Once you're running, the alternator (or generator on older twins) generates AC current, which gets rectified to DC to charge the battery and power the system. Bus bars are just distribution points—think of them as electrical junctions where power splits off to different circuits. A ground power unit (GPU) does the same job as your battery for cold starts, especially useful in winter at places like Christchurch. Over-voltage protection is critical because if your alternator fails in a way that causes over-charging, you'll cook your battery and damage sensitive avionics. That's why you've got a voltage regulator watching the output.
exam_tip: Remember: battery starts and backs up; alternator powers and charges; bus bars distribute; GPU is external battery; voltage regulator prevents over-charge. Be ready to explain why over-voltage protection matters (equipment damage, battery boil-off).
key_facts: Battery provides starting power and emergency backup
Alternator/generator produces main electrical power in flight
Bus bars are distribution centres for electrical loads
Ground power unit (GPU) provides external power for starting
Voltage regulator prevents over-voltage damage to equipment
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Given appropriate data, determine the duration of battery operation following a generator failure.
concept: If your alternator fails mid-flight, you're running on battery power alone—and that battery's capacity is finite. You've got your battery amp-hour rating (typically 25-35 Ah on a light twin) and the aircraft's electrical load in amps. Simple division: capacity divided by load equals remaining time. So if you've got a 30 Ah battery and your electrical load is pulling 40 amps (lights, radio, instruments), you've got roughly 45 minutes before that battery's flat. But here's the reality: you'll start dropping non-essential loads, shed the landing light, maybe dim the panel—so you can stretch it. The exam will give you specific numbers, so the maths is straightforward, but the practical lesson is urgent: get the aircraft on the ground at the nearest suitable airfield.
exam_tip: Use the formula: time (hours) = battery capacity (Ah) ÷ electrical load (amps). In the real world, you'll reduce non-essential loads immediately after an alternator failure and declare an emergency with ATC.
key_facts: Battery capacity is rated in amp-hours (Ah)
Calculate remaining time: capacity ÷ load = hours
Typical light twin battery: 25–35 Ah
Cockpit load in normal flight: 20–50 amps depending on configuration
Reduce non-essential loads immediately (landing lights, autopilot, non-essential avionics)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the relative advantages and disadvantages of: (a) a generator; (b) an alternator.
concept: Generators are the old-school option: they're DC straight out, they're robust, and they tolerate overloads better. But they're heavier, bulkier, and they drop output as engine RPM drops—bad news for idling and low-speed flight. Alternators are lighter, more efficient, and produce constant voltage output across a wide RPM range, which is why modern aircraft use them. They can handle high electrical loads at any engine speed. The downside? They're more sensitive to overloads and high ambient temperatures. On a long hot day flying out of Queenstown with everything running, an alternator will work harder, but it'll work. A generator might drop out on you at low RPM, and that's a headache you don't need on approach.
exam_tip: Generators: robust, older, RPM-dependent output, heavier. Alternators: modern, efficient, constant output, lighter, temperature-sensitive. Know why you'd choose each.
key_facts: Generators produce DC directly; alternators produce AC then rectify to DC
Generators output decreases with lower engine RPM
Alternators maintain constant voltage across wide RPM range
Alternators are lighter and more efficient
Generators better tolerate electrical overloads; alternators more sensitive
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the functions and interpretation of: (a) left-zero ammeters; (b) centre-zero ammeters.
concept: An ammeter tells you whether your electrical system is charging or discharging. A left-zero ammeter has zero on the left, negative (discharge) on the left, and positive (charge) on the right. When you're in flight with the alternator running normally, the needle should sit somewhere on the positive side—usually around +20 to +40 amps depending on the load. A centre-zero ammeter is symmetrical: zero in the middle, discharge to the left, charge to the right. Some twins have this setup. The key thing is: if you see a persistent discharge on either gauge during normal flight, your alternator's not keeping up or it's failed. And if the needle jumps erratically, you've got a problem brewing. Watch it on startup—there's always a brief charge surge as the battery tops up.
exam_tip: Left-zero: zero on left side; centre-zero: zero in centre. Know which one indicates charge (positive) and discharge (negative), and what normal inflight reading looks like (small positive charge).
key_facts: Left-zero ammeter: negative left, zero left edge, positive right
Centre-zero ammeter: negative left, zero centre, positive right
In normal flight: small positive charge (+10 to +40 amps typical)
Discharge indication during flight means alternator failure or overload
Erratic needle movement indicates system malfunction
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the functions and correct operation of a: (a) single battery master switch; (b) split battery/alternator switch.
concept: A single battery master switch is simple: it disconnects the entire electrical system from the battery when you switch it off. That's your primary control—kill it before you leave the aircraft, otherwise you'll come back to a flat battery. A split battery/alternator switch is more sophisticated and found on some twins. You can shut down the alternator independently while keeping the battery online, or vice versa. Why would you do this? If your alternator's running rough and drawing excessive current, you can switch it out and go battery-only until you land. Or if you've got an electrical fire or short circuit, you might kill the alternator but keep the essential battery-backed systems alive long enough to get down safely. The split switch gives you more control, but single switches are easier to manage—fewer decisions in an emergency.
exam_tip: Single switch: kills everything. Split switch: allows independent control of battery and alternator. Know when you'd use each in an electrical emergency (fire, alternator failure, over-charge condition).
key_facts: Single battery master: one switch cuts all electrical power
Split switch: independently control battery and alternator circuits
Always OFF before leaving the aircraft
Split switch useful for electrical fire or alternator malfunction
Some aircraft have battery-only essential bus for critical systems
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the operation of: (a) fuses; (b) circuit breakers; (c) overload switches.
concept: Fuses are one-shot protectors: wire them into a circuit, and if the current exceeds the fuse rating, the wire melts and the circuit opens. Once it's blown, you need a new fuse. Circuit breakers are reusable: they have a bimetal strip that bends with heat from excess current and pops open automatically. Cool it down, hit the reset button, and you're back in business—assuming the problem's fixed. Overload switches are similar but designed for specific applications like the starter motor: they'll cut out if current spikes too high. The philosophy is protection: you don't want a short circuit melting through your wing harness and starting a fire. A blown fuse or popped breaker is telling you something's wrong; don't just keep resetting and hoping—investigate.
exam_tip: Fuses: one-time use, melt on overload. Breakers: reusable, pop open, can reset. Never swap a fuse for a higher amp rating or bypass it. If it keeps popping, there's a fault—don't ignore it.
key_facts: Fuses: disposable wire element that melts on overcurrent
Circuit breakers: reusable, reset manually or automatically
Overload switches protect high-current circuits (starter)
Each circuit protected by correctly-rated fuse or breaker
Repeated fuse blows indicate underlying electrical fault
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain operational principles for the handling of the electrical DC system, including: (a) avoiding overheating electrical services if operated during pre-flight; (b) not starting or stopping the engine with unnecessary electrical equipment switched on; (c) avoiding prolonged use of the starter motor; (d) checking satisfactory operation of the alternator/generator after start, and periodically during flight; (e) ensuring the battery master is switched off before vacating the aircraft after flight; (f) use of a ground power unit.
concept: Here's the practical stuff you'll actually do every flight. During pre-flight, don't run the avionics and lights for ages on battery power alone—you'll heat things up and flatten the battery before you even start. When you're starting the engine, kill the unnecessary stuff: strobes, autopilot, non-essential avionics. Let the starter do its job without fighting to supply 10 other systems. Don't hold the starter engaged for more than 30 seconds at a time—you'll fry it and the battery. As soon as the engine fires and stabilises, check your ammeter: you should see a positive charge, meaning the alternator's doing its job. Keep an eye on it in cruise and before descent too. Before you hop out after landing, flip the battery master to OFF—leave it on and you'll come back to a completely flat battery. And if you're dealing with a cold-soak start in winter at Christchurch or Hamilton, a GPU gets you warm electrons without hammering your onboard battery.
exam_tip: Pre-flight: minimal electrical load on battery. Startup: non-essentials OFF, starter no more than 30 seconds, check ammeter immediately. Landing: switch OFF before you go. Know why each step matters.
key_facts: Limit pre-flight electrical load to protect battery state
Reduce electrical load before engine start (landing lights, autopilot, non-essentials OFF)
Starter motor: maximum 30 seconds continuous use
Check ammeter shows positive charge within 30 seconds of start
Periodic inflight check of alternator output and system voltage
Always switch battery master to OFF before leaving the aircraft
GPU reduces battery stress in cold weather or repeated starts
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the identification of: (a) an excessive charge rate; (b) alternator/generator failure; (c) a total electric failure.
concept: An excessive charge rate—usually more than 50–60 amps on a normal twin—tells you your voltage regulator's not working or the alternator's in a fault mode. Your ammeter will show a sustained high charge, and if you let it run, it'll cook your battery and fry your avionics. Alternator failure is usually obvious: ammeter swings to a discharge reading and stays there, your lights dim, and your battery voltage starts dropping. You'll see it within minutes of the failure. Total electrical failure is the worst case: ammeter dead, flight instruments slowly failing, no radio, nothing. That means both the alternator and battery are offline or the main bus is disconnected. In all cases, you need to declare an emergency, get to the nearest suitable aerodrome, and land as soon as safely possible. You'll need to navigate by compass, fly basic instruments, and manage without radio—not ideal, but it's doable.
exam_tip: Excessive charge: ammeter high (>50A), voltage rising dangerously. Alternator failure: ammeter discharge, dimming lights, sinking voltage. Total failure: nothing works electrically. Each has different implications for your emergency procedures.
key_facts: Excessive charge rate: >50–60 amps sustained charge is abnormal
Alternator failure: ammeter shows discharge, lights dim, voltage drops
Total electrical failure: no instruments, no radio, compass-only navigation
Each failure has distinct ammeter and lighting indications
Declare emergency with ATC immediately upon detection
Land at nearest suitable aerodrome without delay
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the risks associated with: (a) resetting blown fuse/popped circuit breakers; (b) using fuses as a switch.
concept: Resetting a fuse or breaker without investigating the underlying fault is like putting a plaster over a broken bone. You'll get airborne, and that fault—a short circuit, an overloaded wire, whatever it is—is still there. You hit cruise altitude, and suddenly the whole system fails or something catches fire. A popped breaker or blown fuse is a symptom; you need to diagnose and fix the cause, not just reset and hope. Using a fuse as a switch is even worse: fuses aren't designed to open and close repeatedly. The contacts degrade, you can cause arcing, and you'll develop resistance in the circuit which causes heating. Plus, a deliberate fuse operation might not give you a clean open—it can stick or partially engage. Always use the proper switch for on/off control.
exam_tip: Never reset a blown fuse without diagnosing the problem. Never use a fuse as a switch. Both practices risk fire, electrical failure, and aircraft loss.
key_facts: Blown fuses/popped breakers indicate an underlying electrical fault
Resetting without investigation leads to recurrence and potential fire risk
Fuses degrade with repeated opening and closing
Using fuses as switches causes contact damage and resistance heating
Always diagnose the root cause before resetting a protection device
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the issues related specifically to 'technically enhanced aircraft' (TEA) during the handling of electrical malfunctions.
concept: Technically enhanced aircraft—modern twins with glass cockpits, all-electric flight controls, sophisticated avionics—are far more vulnerable to electrical failure than older, simpler types. You lose electrical power and you've potentially lost your entire instrument stack, autopilot, trim control, maybe even your landing gear (depending on the design). A simple electrical glitch that might've been a minor annoyance on a mechanical-instrument Cessna becomes a life-threatening emergency on a Cirrus or modern Piper. You've got redundancy in the avionics—battery-backed systems, standby instruments—but you need to know what you've got and how to use it. Some TEA aircraft have glass displays that need electrical power to show you anything, even standby mode. You must be proficient in manual flying, understanding your fallback options, and knowing which systems degrade gracefully and which fail all at once. In NZ airspace, the CAA and your operating manual will specify what electrical failures require you to land immediately versus which you can manage for a period.
exam_tip: TEA aircraft have higher electrical dependencies. Know your aircraft's specific fallback systems, standby instruments, and procedures. A full electrical failure in a glass-cockpit twin is more serious than in older types.
key_facts: TEA aircraft depend heavily on electrical systems for flight control and avionics
Loss of main electrical power can disable glass displays entirely
Standby instruments and battery-backed systems are critical fallback
Some systems (landing gear, trim) may lose function on electrical failure
Pilot proficiency in manual flying and fallback procedures is essential
Electrical failures in TEA aircraft may require immediate landing
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between gravity-fed and pump-fed fuel systems.
concept: Gravity-fed systems rely on gravity alone: fuel sits high in a header tank or wing tanks positioned above the engine, and fuel simply flows down into the carburettor or fuel manifold. Simple, reliable, few moving parts. The downside? You're limited in what attitudes you can fly and how much negative G you can handle before fuel starvation. Many older singles and some twins use gravity feed. Pump-fed systems use an electric or engine-driven pump to push fuel from the tanks to the engine, regardless of aircraft attitude. This means you can fly inverted (well, aerobatic types can), you've got more flexibility in tank positioning, and you've got better control over fuel flow. Modern twins almost always use pump-fed systems because they give you fuel injection, better flow control, and the ability to manage multiple tanks independently. The trade-off is complexity: pump failure means you need a backup pump or you're in trouble.
exam_tip: Gravity feed: simple, attitude-limited, no pump. Pump-fed: flexible attitudes, complex, pump-dependent. Know which type your aircraft uses and what happens if the pump fails.
key_facts: Gravity-fed: fuel flows by gravity from high tanks to engine
Pump-fed: electric or engine-driven pump pressurises fuel supply
Gravity-feed limits aircraft attitude (no sustained inverted flight)
Pump-fed allows flexible tank positioning and sustained negative-G
Pump failure in pump-fed system is a critical malfunction
Most modern twins use pump-fed systems
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: For a typical fuel system, explain the function, and where appropriate, the correct handling of the following: (a) fuel strainers and filters; (b) engine-driven fuel pump; (c) boost (auxiliary) pump(s); (d) engine primers and priming systems.
concept: Fuel strainers and filters catch contaminants—water, rust, debris—before they reach the injectors or carburettor. On your pre-flight, you'll drain the fuel sampler at the lowest point of the system to check for water. A clogged fuel filter will starve the engine; a dirty one will just reduce power gradually. Engine-driven pumps (mechanical or electric) do the heavy lifting: they pull fuel from the tanks and push it at the right pressure to the engine. Boost pumps (auxiliary pumps) are electric pumps that sit in the tanks themselves.
