You run them before takeoff to prime the fuel system and ensure positive pressure to the engine-driven pump—especially important on hot days or at altitude where vapour lock can occur. They're also your backup if the main pump fails. Primers inject raw fuel directly into the cylinders for cold starts—you'll prime before cranking in winter. Some systems use pump priming instead, where the boost pump pressurises the system before you hit the starter.
exam_tip: Filters: check pre-flight, replace if clogged. Engine pump: main supply. Boost pump: ON before takeoff, maintains prime, backup if main pump fails. Primer: ON before cold start, OFF after engine fires.
key_facts: Fuel strainers catch contaminants; drain sample pre-flight to check for water
Clogged fuel filter causes engine starvation
Engine-driven pump provides main fuel pressure
Boost pump primes system before takeoff and provides backup supply
Boost pump essential on hot days and at high altitude (vapour lock prevention)
Primer injects raw fuel into cylinders for cold starts
Boost pump also used to maintain pressure if engine pump fails
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: For a fuel system, explain the function, and where appropriate, the correct handling of the following: (a) tank filler caps and drains; (b) expansion spaces; (c) tank vents; (d) baffles; (e) sumps and drains; (f) fuel quantity detectors; (g) fuel strainers and filters; (h) tank selector valves; (i) cross feed valves; (j) fuel flow meters.
concept: Tank filler caps seal the system and prevent contamination during refuelling—use the correct cap for each tank and check the O-ring seals. Tank drains are at the lowest point; you'll open them during pre-flight to sample fuel and check for water and sediment. Expansion spaces let fuel grow as temperature rises without rupturing the tank—that's why you don't fill tanks completely on a hot day. Tank vents allow air in as fuel is consumed so you don't create a vacuum that collapses the tank or starves the engine. Baffles are internal dividers that prevent fuel sloshing around in rough air and keep the fuel pickup submerged even in a dive or steep turn. Sumps are collection points at the tank bottom where sediment and water settle; you drain these during pre-flight. Fuel quantity detectors (usually floats) tell you how much fuel's in each tank—check them on the ground and monitor them in flight. Tank selectors let you choose which tank feeds the engine; cross-feed valves let you transfer fuel between left and right tanks if one's running low or if an engine fails. Fuel flow meters show fuel consumption rate—essential for managing endurance and detecting abnormal consumption.
exam_tip: Know each component's function and when to check it (pre-flight sampling, tank selection during flight, monitoring quantity). Understand why expansion space matters and what happens if vents are blocked.
key_facts: Filler caps: sealed, O-ring seals checked, correct cap per tank
Tank drains: lowest point, sampled pre-flight for water and sediment
Expansion space: allows fuel thermal expansion without tank rupture
Tank vents: prevent vacuum and maintain pressure as fuel is used
Baffles: prevent fuel sloshing, keep pickup submerged in manoeuvres
Sumps: collection points for sediment and water, drained pre-flight
Quantity detectors: float types, check ground and monitor in flight
Tank selector: choose fuel source; cross-feed: transfer between tanks
Fuel flow meter: shows consumption rate for endurance planning
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe: (a) the general rules for refuelling; (b) the correct use of fuel tank dipsticks; (c) the correct use of magna-sticks.
concept: Refuelling rules: aircraft nose into wind to prevent fuel spray, engine off (obviously), master switch off to prevent sparks, ground the refuelling cart and aeroplane to prevent static discharge. Fill on a level surface and don't overfill—leave the expansion space. Check the fuel type matches your engine (Mogas vs avgas, octane rating, etc.). Dipsticks are old-school but still used: you insert them into the filler neck until they bottom, then pull them out and read the fuel level against the graduated marks. They're accurate but they're a physical inspection, not electronic—you've got to do it. Magna-sticks (or fuel strut sticks) are plastic dipsticks with floating magnets inside. You fill the stick with a sample of fuel from the tank drain, and magnets at different levels show you the water level. If water's present, it settles at the bottom and the magnet sinks to it. The marks tell you the fuel grade and any water contamination. It's faster than waiting for sediment to settle, and it gives you confidence the fuel's clean. Some FBOs still prefer the traditional jar method—letting fuel sit in a clear container so water settles visibly—but magna-sticks are becoming standard.
exam_tip: Refuelling: level surface, nose into wind, ground the aircraft, no spark sources, correct fuel type. Dipstick: insert fully, read graduated marks. Magna-stick: fill from tank sample, note magnet position for water and fuel grade.
key_facts: Refuelling procedure: level surface, nose into wind, engines off, master off
Ground aircraft and refuelling cart to prevent static discharge
Confirm fuel type and octane rating before refuelling
Don't overfill; leave expansion space
Dipstick: insert to bottom, read graduated scale on withdrawal
Magna-stick: fill with fuel sample, magnets show water level and fuel grade
Fuel should be clear and bright; contamination requires tank draining
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the correct procedures to be used for carrying out fuel quality checks.
concept: Fuel quality checks are simple but non-negotiable. At refuelling, you've already verified the grade. On pre-flight, you'll drain a sample from the lowest sump point of each tank into a clear container (usually a fuel sampler cup at the refuelling point). Let it sit for a few seconds; look for water settling at the bottom—it'll be cloudy or a distinct layer. Check for sediment (particles, rust, debris) which should be absent. The fuel should be clear and bright, with the correct colour for the grade: Avgas 100LL is typically light blue, Avgas 100 is green, Jet A is clear to pale straw. Any discolouration, cloudiness, or sediment means the tank needs flushing or fuel dumping. On a multi-tank aircraft like a twin, check every tank—you might find contamination in one wing tank but not the other. If you find water, don't fly; declare the aircraft unserviceable and contact maintenance. This isn't a shortcut—it's the difference between a normal flight and an engine failure at low level.
exam_tip: Sample every tank pre-flight. Look for water (cloudiness at bottom), sediment (particles), and correct colour. Cloudy fuel = no-go. Know the colour standards for fuel grades used in NZ.
key_facts: Drain fuel sample from lowest sump point of each tank
Sample into clear container; let settle briefly
Look for water (cloudiness), sediment (particles), and correct colour
Avgas 100LL: light blue; Avgas 100: green; Jet A: clear to straw
Any cloudiness or sediment means tank requires maintenance
Check every tank on multi-tank aircraft
Fuel contamination is a no-go condition
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the indications of water in a fuel sample.
concept: Water in fuel is the enemy. It'll corrode fuel system components, it'll plug injectors and filters, and it can freeze in the fuel lines at altitude, blocking fuel flow mid-flight. When you drain a fuel sample and water's present, you'll see a distinct layer at the bottom of the container—it looks cloudy or milky if it's emulsified (mixed with fuel), or it might be a clear layer below the fuel. Tiny droplets suspended throughout the fuel make it look hazy or turbid. On a hot day, you might see no water initially, but as the sample cools in your hand, water that was vapour condenses out and becomes visible. If you let the sample sit for a minute, water will settle to the bottom and you'll see it clearly. The colour changes too: normal fuel is bright and clear; fuel with water is dull, cloudy, or has a distinct separation. In serious contamination, you might even smell the water—it has that distinct odour. Don't take any chances: if you see water, the aircraft doesn't fly, and you need to get maintenance involved to find the source (usually a leaking fuel cap or a tank vent failure in damp conditions).
exam_tip: Water indicators: cloudiness, haze, distinct bottom layer, colour change (dull instead of bright). Let sample sit to let water settle. Any visible water = no-go.
key_facts: Water appears as cloudiness, haze, or distinct layer at bottom of sample
Water may be emulsified (mixed throughout) or separated (bottom layer)
Cool sample in hand; water vapour condenses as temperature drops
Fuel with water looks dull or turbid instead of bright and clear
Serious contamination has visible water column at bottom
Water in fuel risks filter blockage, injector damage, and fuel line freeze
Any visible water is a no-go condition
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the symptoms of a blocked fuel tank vent in a gravity feed fuel system.
concept: A blocked vent in a gravity-feed system creates a vacuum in the tank as fuel is consumed. The engine will run fine at first, but as fuel level drops, the vacuum gets stronger and eventually strangles the fuel flow. You'll notice the engine running rough, losing power, and if it's bad enough, cutting out completely. The problem is it's intermittent—climb or descend slightly, maybe the siphoning effect changes, and fuel flows again. You might chalk it up to carburettor ice or fuel starvation from leaning the mixture. But the real giveaway is that fuel quantity gauge starts fluctuating wildly because the float is bouncing around as the vacuum sucks it. If you suspect a blocked vent mid-flight, you might try a gentle climb or descent to change the pressure differential, or you might open a cabin air vent to let air into the cabin, which could relieve some back-pressure. But really, it's a land-and-sort-it situation. On the ground, check that the vent isn't clogged with dirt, ice, or a blocked valve.
exam_tip: Blocked vent symptoms: engine rough or cutting out as fuel level drops, fluctuating fuel gauge, progressive power loss. On the ground, check vent for blockage or ice.
key_facts: Blocked vent creates vacuum as fuel is consumed
Engine roughness and power loss as vacuum builds
Fuel quantity gauge fluctuates erratically
Problem worsens as fuel level drops (more vacuum)
Changing altitude may temporarily relieve symptom
Vents can block with dirt, ice, or internal failure
Land immediately if blocked vent suspected
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the importance of correct management of fuel selection.
concept: On a twin-engine aeroplane, you've got multiple fuel tanks—typically left wing, right wing, and maybe a centre tank. You've got a selector valve on the flight deck that lets you choose which tank feeds each engine. Get this wrong and you'll have an engine failure, or worse, an asymmetric thrust situation where one engine's running and the other's starved. Proper fuel management means: know your tank capacities, monitor which tank is supplying each engine, understand your fuel consumption rates, and plan your takeoff sequence. Some aircraft require you to burn from specific tanks in a specific order to maintain centre of gravity. If you're flying out of Hamilton and you've got an asymmetric load (say, full right tank and half-full left), you need to be deliberate about which tank feeds which engine so you don't end up with both engines trying to feed from the depleted side. On some operations, you'll cross-feed in flight to balance consumption. And critically, if one engine dies, you need to immediately switch that engine to an alternate tank (if available) or declare the situation and plan your approach. Sloppy fuel selection has killed a lot of pilots—it's not a details-to-ignore kind of thing.
exam_tip: Know your tank layout and capacities. Plan takeoff fuel sequence. Monitor consumption. Maintain CG balance. If one engine dies, switch to alternate fuel source immediately. Document fuel selection in your pre-flight checklist.
key_facts: Multi-tank aircraft require deliberate tank selection
Wrong selection causes single-engine failure or dual failure
Tank selector valves must be properly identified and used
Fuel consumption must be monitored to maintain aircraft balance
Asymmetric fuel loads require cross-feed planning
Engine failure due to fuel selection requires immediate alternate tank selection
Fuel management is part of pre-takeoff and in-flight checklist
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Detail the actions recommended in the case of loss of power through faulty fuel selection.
concept: You've selected the wrong tank, one engine's lost power, and suddenly you're flying an asymmetric twin—not a fun situation. First thing: don't panic. Immediately check your fuel selector: it's probably in the wrong position. Switch it to the correct tank, and usually the engine will pick up within 5–10 seconds as fuel pressure builds. If the engine doesn't respond, try the opposite tank if you've got alternatives, or try your crossfeed valve if available. If you've got an engine failure that you can't recover—the selector's already in the right position and fuel's available elsewhere—then you're declaring a single-engine emergency with ATC. You've lost asymmetric thrust, so trim carefully, reduce power on the good engine to maintain control, and plan your descent to the nearest suitable aerodrome. Call it early so ATC can vector you and prepare the airfield. Don't try to climb or maintain altitude on one engine in a twin with a significant loading—you'll just burn fuel inefficiently and stress the airframe. Get down safely.
exam_tip: Wrong fuel selection = engine loss. Immediately check and correct selector position. Verify fuel's actually in that tank (gauge might show empty). If engine doesn't recover, declare emergency and plan landing at nearest suitable aerodrome. Manage asymmetric thrust carefully.
key_facts: Check fuel selector immediately when engine power is lost
Switch to correct tank; engine usually responds within 5–10 seconds
Confirm fuel exists in the selected tank (check gauge)
Try crossfeed if available
If unrecovered, declare single-engine emergency
Plan descent to nearest suitable aerodrome
Manage asymmetric thrust: reduce good engine power, trim carefully
Don't attempt to climb on one engine unnecessarily
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the typical methods of cross-feeding fuel.
concept: Cross-feeding is transferring fuel from one tank to another, usually from a wing tank to the opposite wing tank. On many twins, you've got a crossfeed selector valve that lets you feed a single engine from the opposite wing tank. Why would you do this? If one wing tank's running low and the other's still full, you can cross-feed to balance consumption and maintain centre of gravity. If an engine's on the verge of failure and you've got fuel in the opposite tank, you can try cross-feeding to see if the problem's a blocked feed line instead of total fuel starvation. Some aircraft have manual crossfeed valves; others have electric selectors on the panel. The procedure is simple: move the crossfeed selector to 'on' or to the desired position, monitor that the engine stays running, and watch your fuel gauges to ensure you're actually consuming from the opposite tank. Cross-feeding during cruise helps balance aircraft loading on long flights. Some operations require specific cross-feed sequences—check your flight manual. And remember: you can only cross-feed if you've got fuel in both tanks and the crossfeed system is serviceable. You can't pull fuel from an empty tank.
exam_tip: Crossfeed transfers fuel between left and right tanks. Used to balance consumption and maintain CG. Operate selector, monitor engine and fuel gauges. Confirm fuel actually transfers. Know your aircraft's crossfeed valve location and operation.
key_facts: Crossfeed transfers fuel between wing tanks to maintain balance
Valve is either manual or electric selector on flight deck
Monitor fuel gauges to confirm crossfeed operation
Engine should stay running; observe no fluctuation
Used during cruise for extended flights to manage CG
Can help if one feed line is blocked (try crossfeed to opposite tank)
Requires fuel in both tanks; can't transfer from empty tank
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the main functions of an engine oil system.
concept: Engine oil does three main jobs: it lubricates all the moving parts—bearings, pistons, crankshaft, camshaft—so they slide smoothly without excessive friction and heat. Second, it cools components by carrying heat away from the hottest parts (like the piston underside) back to the cooler sump and then the oil cooler. Third, it cleans: the oil circulates through the engine, suspending dirt and contaminants, carrying them to the filter where they're trapped. As the oil ages and accumulates sludge, it loses all these properties, which is why regular oil changes are essential. On a piston twin sitting at Ardmore in summer heat, the oil's working hard—if you let it get too hot or too thick with gunk, you'll see rising oil temperature and falling pressure, and eventually you'll have bearing damage. That's unrecoverable.
exam_tip: Oil lubricates, cools, and cleans. Pressure indicates flow; temperature indicates heat load. Monitor both continuously. Low pressure or high temperature during flight means land soon.
key_facts: Oil lubricates engine bearings, pistons, and rotating parts
Oil transfers heat from hot engine components to cooler sump
Oil suspends contaminants and carries them to the filter
Regular oil changes remove accumulated sludge and contaminants
Oil viscosity changes with temperature; thicker when cold, thinner when hot
Low oil pressure indicates poor lubrication; high temperature indicates overload
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect of temperature on the viscosity and lubrication qualities of oil.
concept: Oil viscosity is its thickness, and it changes dramatically with temperature. Cold oil is thick and sluggish—on a winter morning at Christchurch, you might have to wait longer before the oil pressure stabilises because thick oil doesn't flow easily through the bearings. Hot oil is thin and watery—high altitude or a long descent in summer heat makes the oil fluid, which is good for flow but bad for the film between bearing surfaces. The viscosity needs to be in a sweet spot: thick enough to maintain a protective film between bearings (low viscosity = poor lubrication = bearing wear), thin enough to flow and circulate (high viscosity = poor flow = pressure drop and cooling problems). That's why you use specific oil grades: a SAE 40 oil stays relatively consistent across temperature ranges, while a SAE 10 oil is thin and flows better in winter but might be too thin in summer. Modern ashless dispersant oils (used in aviation piston engines) maintain viscosity better across temperature swings than straight mineral oil, which is why you use them.
exam_tip: Cold oil: thick, high pressure initially, slow to warm. Hot oil: thin, low pressure, poor bearing film. Know your oil grade and acceptable pressure/temperature ranges. Monitor both gauges.
key_facts: Oil thickens when cold; thins when hot
Cold engine: high oil pressure, long warm-up needed
Hot engine: low oil pressure, risk of bearing damage
Oil viscosity must balance flow (thin) and bearing protection (thick)
SAE ratings (10, 30, 40, etc.) define oil weight
Multi-grade oils (e.g., 15W-50) adapt to temperature changes
Ashless dispersant oils maintain viscosity better than mineral oils
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Compare the features of the ashless dispersant (AD) oils used in piston aero-engines with straight mineral oil and detergent oils.
concept: Ashless dispersant oils are the gold standard for piston aero-engines. They don't leave ash residue when they burn (minerals and detergents do), which means cleaner combustion, less sludge buildup, and longer engine life. The 'dispersant' part means they hold contaminants in suspension so they don't settle and form gunk—they stay suspended until the filter catches them. Straight mineral oil is basic: it lubricates but it leaves ash, it doesn't hold contaminants well, and it breaks down faster. It's outdated. Detergent oils (used in cars) have additives that clean engine deposits, which sounds good, but in aircraft piston engines, they can cause issues: they leave ash residue, they react with aviation fuel additives in ways we don't want, and they're not certified for use in aircraft. Your flight manual will specify ashless dispersant (usually Tempus, Aeroshell, or equivalent). Don't deviate—mixing oils or using the wrong grade can void your engine warranty and potentially damage the engine. The AD oils are more expensive, but they're designed specifically for our engines and they extend time between overhauls.
exam_tip: Use only ashless dispersant oils in piston aircraft engines. Never mix grades or use automotive detergent oils. AD oils: no ash, hold contaminants, longer intervals. Know the brand and grade specified in your POH.
key_facts: Ashless dispersant (AD) oils are approved for piston aero-engines
AD oils don't leave ash residue; combustion is cleaner
Dispersant additive suspends contaminants for filter removal
Straight mineral oils leave ash and break down faster (obsolete)
Detergent oils (automotive) are not approved for aircraft use
Mixing oil grades or types is not permitted
AD oils extend time between engine overhauls
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: With respect to oil grades, differentiate between the commercial aviation numbers and SAE ratings.
concept: You'll see two numbering systems on oil bottles. Commercial aviation numbers like 15, 30, 40, 50 are what the aero industry uses—they're based on viscosity at specific temperatures. SAE (Society of Automotive Engineers) numbers—SAE 10, 20, 30, etc.—are similar but they're automotive standards. They roughly correspond: aviation 30 ≈ SAE 20–30W, aviation 40 ≈ SAE 40, etc. But here's the key: don't assume they're identical. Your aircraft POH will specify aviation numbers, and that's what you use. If it says 'aero oil 50', you buy aviation oil 50, not SAE 50W. The 'W' in automotive oils (15W, 20W) means winter grade—it's a multi-viscosity oil that's thin when cold and thick when hot. Aviation oils sometimes use similar naming but the specs are different. The short version: follow your POH exactly. It'll say something like 'Tempus aero oil 40' or 'Aeroshell 15W-50'—use exactly that. Don't improvise.
exam_tip: Aviation oil numbers (15, 30, 40, 50) ≠ exact SAE equivalents. Follow your POH specification exactly. Don't mix grades or substitute automotive oils. Know what's written in your aircraft manual.
key_facts: Aviation oil grades: 15, 30, 40, 50 based on aero specs
SAE grades: 10, 20, 30, 40, etc. based on automotive standards
Roughly correspond but are not identical
Multi-grade oils: SAE 15W-50, for example, adapt to temperature
Always use the exact grade specified in the POH
Never substitute or mix grades without explicit approval
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Differentiate between a wet sump and a dry sump oil system.
concept: A wet sump system is simple: all the oil sits in a pan bolted to the bottom of the engine. The engine-driven pump pulls oil from this pan, circulates it through the engine, and it drains back into the same pan. It's straightforward and low-maintenance, but it's limited by aircraft attitude—if you dive steeply or fly inverted, the oil sloshes around and you risk oil starvation because the pickup tube loses suction. Most piston twins use wet sump because they're not aerobatic. A dry sump system is more complex: there's a small oil tank mounted separately, usually in a wing or fuselage location. The engine pump draws from this tank and circulates through the engine, then a scavenge pump pulls the used oil back out of the engine sump into a separate return tank. This setup means oil stays isolated, independent of aircraft attitude, so you can fly inverted, barrel roll, whatever. Aerobatic aircraft and some high-performance types use dry sump. It's heavier, more complex, and needs more maintenance. For CPL exams in NZ, know the difference and understand why your aircraft has the one it has.
exam_tip: Wet sump: simple, oil pan bolted to engine, altitude-sensitive. Dry sump: separate tank, scavenge pump, altitude-independent. Most twins = wet sump. Aerobatic = dry sump.
key_facts: Wet sump: oil pan at engine bottom, pump pulls and drains to same pan
Wet sump limited to shallow manoeuvres (no sustained inverted flight)
Dry sump: separate oil tank and return system with scavenge pump
Dry sump allows any aircraft attitude including inverted flight
Dry sump heavier and more complex than wet sump
Most piston twins use wet sump systems
Aerobatic and high-performance aircraft use dry sump
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of: (a) the engine-driven oil pump and pressure relief valve; (b) oil lines, passages and galleries; (c) oil sumps, and scavenge pumps; (d) oil cooler; (e) oil tank and filter.
concept: The engine-driven pump is a gear pump or rotary pump that's mechanically driven by the engine—as engine speed increases, pump output increases, delivering more oil pressure. The pressure relief valve prevents over-pressure: if pressure climbs too high (usually set around 60–80 psi), the relief opens and dumps excess oil back to the sump. Oil lines carry oil from the pump to galleries in the engine block. Galleries are internal passages casting through the engine, distributing oil to every bearing and critical surface. The sump is the oil pan at the engine bottom where used oil drains and collects for recirculation. In a dry sump system, a scavenge pump pulls used oil from the engine sump into a separate return tank. The oil cooler is a heat exchanger (like a small radiator) where oil passes through cooling fins and sheds heat to airflow or ram air. The oil tank holds the oil supply and provides residence time for contaminants to settle before recirculation. The filter traps debris—you'll change it at specified intervals (usually every 50–100 hours on a piston twin) and check it during pre-flight.
exam_tip: Pump: mechanical drive, delivers pressure. Relief: prevents over-pressure. Galleries: internal passages. Cooler: sheds heat. Filter: traps debris. Monitor pressure and temperature continuously.
key_facts: Engine-driven pump: gear or rotary, output increases with RPM
Pressure relief valve opens at ~60–80 psi
Oil galleries: internal passages distributing oil through engine block
Oil sump: collection pan at engine bottom
Scavenge pump (dry sump only): returns used oil to tank
Oil cooler: heat exchanger shedding heat to airflow
Oil filter: captures debris, replaced every 50–100 hours
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the need for periodic oil changes.
concept: Oil degrades with use. The ashless dispersant additive gets depleted, the oil picks up moisture and contaminants, carbon buildup increases, and eventually the oil's no longer effective at lubrication and cooling. Typically, you'll change piston engine oil every 50–100 hours (your POH specifies exactly) or annually, whichever comes first. On a twin that's being flown regularly (50+ hours a month), you'll hit the hour limit first. If the aircraft is flown infrequently, oil degrades anyway and you'll hit the annual limit. The oil filter gets changed at every oil change—it's collecting debris, and once it's full of gunk, it restricts flow. Your pre-flight oil check is a visual inspection of quantity only; the contamination and degradation you can't see until you have oil analysed in a lab (which some operators do). Flying with old, degraded oil is like flying with a leaking seal bearing—you're running on borrowed time waiting for bearing failure.
exam_tip: Oil changes at 50–100 hour intervals or annually. Filter changed at every oil change. Old oil = poor lubrication and cooling. Stick to your POH change schedule.
key_facts: Oil degrades with time and use
Ashless dispersant additive depletes gradually
Typical change interval: 50–100 hours or annually
Whichever interval comes first is the limit
Oil filter changed at every oil change
Regular oil sampling can detect contamination early
Flying with expired oil risks bearing damage and engine failure
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the likely results of operating an engine with: (a) incorrect oil type; (b) incorrect oil quantity.
concept: Using the wrong oil type—say, using automotive detergent oil or a different aviation grade than specified—causes several problems. Detergent oils leave ash residue in the combustion chambers, fouling plugs and reducing ignition efficiency. The additive package might not be compatible with avgas, causing sludge or varnish buildup. Wrong aviation grades (too thin or too thick) mean either poor bearing lubrication (thin oil) or clogged filters and pressure problems (thick oil). Operating with insufficient oil (low quantity) means less heat dissipation, higher oil temperature, lower pressure, and risk of bearing starvation. You'll see rising temps and dropping pressure on the gauges. Excess oil is also bad: it foams in the sump, reducing its viscosity, and the extra heat generated by friction and aeration will spike temperature. Your daily oil level check (cold, on a level surface) should show the oil within the green band on the dipstick. Too much or too little is a problem.
exam_tip: Wrong oil type: deposits, sludge, poor lubrication. Too little oil: high temp, low pressure, bearing risk. Too much oil: foaming, high temp. Always use specified grade and maintain level in green band.
key_facts: Wrong oil type causes deposits, sludge, additive incompatibility
Detergent (automotive) oils leave ash residue (not approved)
Wrong aviation grade: poor flow or poor lubrication
Low oil quantity: high temperature, low pressure, bearing starvation
Excess oil: foaming, increased friction, high temperature
Monitor oil level before each flight (cold, level surface)
Maintain level within green band on dipstick
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the likely causes of: (a) low oil pressure; (b) high oil pressure; (c) high oil temperature.
concept: Low oil pressure mid-flight is usually a bearing wear issue: loose bearings have bigger gaps, oil leaks past instead of staying in the bearing film, pressure drops. It can also be a failed pressure relief valve (stuck open), a blocked oil filter restricting return flow, a faulty pump, or actual oil leakage from a line. High oil pressure is less common but usually means a stuck pressure relief valve (stays closed when it should open) or a clogged oil filter causing backpressure. High oil temperature usually comes from high oil load (big power settings, hot day, lots of electrical load or systems drawing power), inadequate cooling (fouled oil cooler, low airflow at low speed), or high friction from low oil quantity or viscosity too thick. Combining low pressure with high temperature is a red flag: it often means bearing wear—the bearing's loose, friction is high, oil's getting hot, pressure's dropping because the bearing isn't holding oil. That's a get-down-now situation. Your oil cooler can get fouled (dust, insects, salt spray if you're operating near the coast at places like Auckland), so check it visually during pre-flight.
exam_tip: Low pressure: bearing wear, pump failure, filter clog, relief stuck open. High pressure: relief stuck closed, filter clog. High temp: overload, poor cooling, low quantity. Low pressure + high temp = bearing wear = land urgently.
key_facts: Low oil pressure: bearing wear, pump failure, filter blockage, relief stuck open
High oil pressure: relief valve stuck closed, filter blockage
High oil temperature: high power setting, hot day, fouled cooler, insufficient quantity
Low pressure + high temperature: bearing wear (critical situation)
Monitor pressure and temperature continuously in flight
Oil cooler can foul from dust, insects, salt spray
Combination of low pressure and high temperature requires immediate landing
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the relationship between a fluctuating or low oil pressure reading accompanied by a rise in oil temperature.
concept: This combination is a red flag that points to bearing wear. Here's the chain of events: as a bearing wears, the clearance between the rotating shaft and the bearing surface increases. Oil that should stay in a thin film between the surfaces starts leaking past, increasing oil flow but reducing the protective film. With less film pressure, the bearing surfaces make closer contact, friction rises, heat generation spikes. You see the oil temperature climbing. Meanwhile, the pressure reading drops because the worn bearing isn't holding pressure—the oil's just flowing through and leaking out. The fluctuation you're seeing is the pressure bouncing around as the bearing rocks or the oil flow becomes intermittent. This is a bearing on the way out. You're not going to recover from this in flight, and continuing to run the engine makes it worse. Declare a precautionary landing immediately and get the aircraft on the ground and into maintenance. Don't power back up until a mechanic has inspected and cleared it.
exam_tip: Low pressure + high temperature + fluctuation = bearing wear. Immediate landing required. Don't continue running. Each minute of flight accelerates bearing damage.
key_facts: Bearing wear increases clearance between shaft and bearing surface
Increased clearance = oil leakage from bearing film
Oil leakage = lower pressure, higher friction, higher temperature
Fluctuating pressure indicates bearing movement or intermittent contact
This combination is a critical failure in progress
Immediate landing required; do not continue flying
Aircraft grounded until maintenance inspection and repair
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the actions the pilot should take in the low pressure/high temperature situation.
concept: You've got low pressure and high temperature gauges. First, verify it's a real problem and not a gauge fault: cross-check with the other engine if it's a twin, or look for other symptoms like roughness or unusual vibration. If both engines show normal readings, suspect the gauge. If it's one engine and real, reduce power immediately to the minimum needed to maintain safe flight and climb (if possible). Reducing load reduces friction and might bring the temperature down and pressure up slightly. Get a descent planned to your nearest suitable aerodrome—you're landing soon. Call ATC immediately and declare a precautionary landing; they'll clear vectors and prepare the field. Don't try to climb to altitude or stretch the glide; just head for the nearest place you can put it down safely. Avoid manoeuvres that increase engine load or G-forces. Once on the ground, ensure the engine is shut down properly and the aircraft is secured. Don't restart it; let maintenance inspect it. If you're at an outstation without immediate maintenance, inform the operator immediately.
exam_tip: Verify real (not gauge fault). Reduce power. Plan descent to nearest suitable airfield. Declare precautionary landing with ATC. No climbing or stretching. Land smoothly and shut down. Notify operator; don't restart.
key_facts: First: verify it's a real problem, not gauge failure
Reduce power immediately to minimum safe level
Plan descent to nearest suitable aerodrome
Declare precautionary landing with ATC early
Avoid manoeuvres increasing engine load
Land and shut down; do not restart
Secure aircraft and notify operator
Maintenance inspection required before further flight
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe: (a) mechanical advantage; (b) how mechanical advantage can be gained hydraulically; (c) the principle of operation of typical aircraft hydraulic services.
concept: Mechanical advantage is the multiplying effect of force. A lever is mechanical advantage—push down on a long arm and lift a heavy load on the short arm. Hydraulically, you gain mechanical advantage through area ratios. If you've got a small pump pushing fluid through a small line into a large actuator cylinder, the pressure's the same throughout, but the force exerted is proportional to area. A pump with a 1-square-inch piston and a large actuator with a 4-square-inch piston gives you a 4:1 mechanical advantage—the pilot's light force input gets amplified into a powerful actuator output. That's why aircraft use hydraulics: to convert pilot input (light forces, small movements) into powerful control surface movement or landing gear extension. A typical system has a pump (driven by the engine or an electric motor), a pressure relief valve (to prevent over-pressure), accumulators (to smooth pressure spikes and provide emergency power), check valves (to prevent backflow), selector valves (to direct flow), and actuators (hydraulic cylinders that do the work: raise landing gear, lower flaps, move flight controls). The fluid is incompressible, so movement is immediate and precise—no slop.
exam_tip: Hydraulic advantage = pressure × area. Small pump input into large actuator = big force output. Systems include pump, relief, accumulators, check valves, selectors, and actuators. No compressibility = precise, immediate movement.
key_facts: Mechanical advantage: force multiplied by area ratio
Hydraulic pump: converts engine/motor power to fluid pressure
Pressure is constant throughout a closed system
Force output = pressure × actuator area
Pump pressure typically 1000–3000 psi in aircraft
Actuators convert hydraulic pressure into linear or rotary motion
Fluid is incompressible; movement is immediate
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the advantages of using hydraulics to operate aircraft services.
concept: Hydraulics are the power distribution system of choice for modern aircraft. Why? First, high power density: you can transmit enormous forces through small tubing without heavy mechanical linkages. Deploying landing gear or lowering full flaps on a twin requires huge forces; hydraulics do it smoothly and efficiently. Second, precision: because fluid is incompressible, movement is repeatable and exact. A hydraulic landing gear extension is always the same; there's no slop or inconsistency. Third, safety: hydraulics can be routed through fireproof bulkheads and isolated with shutoff valves. A mechanical cable goes through the firewall and can corrode or fail; a sealed hydraulic line with isolation capability is safer. Fourth, light weight and space efficiency: compare the plumbing needed for mechanical flight controls (cables, pulleys, levers everywhere) versus a single hydraulic line and a small actuator. Modern aircraft are packed with systems; hydraulics let you fit them efficiently. Fifth, redundancy: you can run multiple hydraulic systems (left and right independent systems on a large twin) so single failure doesn't completely disable the aircraft.
exam_tip: Hydraulics offer: high power, precision, safety, light weight, efficiency, and redundancy. That's why they're universal on aircraft with complex landing gear and surface control systems.
key_facts: Hydraulics provide high force in compact size
Incompressible fluid = precise, repeatable movement
Sealed lines = safer than exposed cables or linkages
Can route through fireproof bulkheads
Light and space-efficient compared to mechanical systems
Allows multiple independent systems for redundancy
Isolatable with shutoff valves for safety
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Differentiate between the three types of hydraulic oil.
concept: There are three main types of hydraulic fluid used in aviation: mineral oil-based fluids (traditional, compatible with most seals), phosphate ester fluids (resistant to fire, used where fire risk is high), and synthetic fluids (improved performance, temperature stability, longer life). Mineral oil is the standard in most general aviation aircraft because it's inexpensive, widely available, and works well with standard seals and components. Phosphate ester is expensive and used mainly in military, transport, and high-risk applications where a hydraulic fire could be catastrophic. Synthetics offer advantages in extreme temperature environments or where long fluid life is valued. Never mix hydraulic fluid types: they have different chemical properties and mixing can cause seal degradation and system failure. Your POH will specify exactly which fluid type your aircraft uses. When servicing hydraulics, use fresh fluid from a sealed container; never reuse or top up without inspection. Hydraulic fluid degrades with contamination and heat, so periodic fluid sampling (on larger aircraft) is standard practice.
exam_tip: Know the three types: mineral (standard, most GA), phosphate ester (fire-resistant, military/transport), synthetic (high-performance). Never mix types. Always use the exact type specified in your POH.
key_facts: Mineral oil-based: standard for GA, inexpensive, compatible with seals
Phosphate ester: fire-resistant, expensive, military/transport use
Synthetic: improved temperature stability, longer life, high-performance
Never mix hydraulic fluid types; causes seal and system damage
Use only fresh fluid from sealed containers
Hydraulic fluid degrades with contamination and heat
Follow POH specification exactly
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of common hydraulic system components including: (a) reservoirs; (b) pumps; (c) pressure regulators; (d) accumulators; (e) check valves and release valves; (f) selector valves; (g) actuators; (h) filters.
concept: The reservoir holds the bulk of the hydraulic fluid, provides a place for air bubbles to escape and contaminants to settle, and dissipates heat. Pumps are engine-driven or electric-driven and convert mechanical power into fluid pressure. Pressure regulators (relief valves) prevent over-pressure by opening if system pressure exceeds a set point (usually 2000–3000 psi), dumping excess flow back to the reservoir. Accumulators are like small pressure vessels that store pressurised fluid—they smooth out pressure spikes, provide emergency power if the pump fails, and absorb shock. Check valves allow flow in one direction only, preventing backflow. Selector (directional control) valves route hydraulic flow to different actuators depending on the pilot's input—left, right, up, down, etc. Release valves (not to be confused with relief valves) are manually operated and allow emergency decompression or manual lowering of systems if hydraulic power is lost. Actuators are the working end: hydraulic cylinders that extend and retract (landing gear, flaps, trim) or rotary actuators that rotate (flight controls). Filters catch contaminants to protect the system and maintain fluid cleanliness.
exam_tip: Each component has a job: reservoir holds fluid, pump pressurises it, regulator prevents over-pressure, accumulator provides smoothing and backup, check valves prevent backflow, selectors route flow, actuators do the work, filters keep it clean.
key_facts: Reservoir: stores fluid, allows air escape, dissipates heat
Pump: converts mechanical power to hydraulic pressure
Pressure regulator (relief): opens if pressure exceeds set point
Accumulator: stores pressure, smooths spikes, provides emergency power
Check valve: allows one-way flow, prevents backflow
Selector valve: directs flow to desired actuator
Release valve: allows manual decompression or emergency lowering
Actuator: hydraulic cylinder or rotary motor that performs work
Filter: removes contaminants, maintains fluid cleanliness
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the operation of the following hydraulic systems: (a) open centre system; (b) pressurised system.
concept: An open centre system is simple: hydraulic fluid is always flowing, circulating from the pump through various selector valves and back to the reservoir, even when no actuators are being commanded. When you want to operate an actuator (say, lower the landing gear), you move the selector valve which directs the flow into the actuator cylinder, pushing it. Excess flow goes back to the reservoir. It's straightforward, no complex regulators, but it's wasteful—the pump is always spinning and generating heat. Pressurised (or closed centre) systems are more efficient: the pump is on demand. Fluid sits idle in the accumulators until the pilot commands an actuator. Moving the selector valve directs pressurised fluid to the actuator and simultaneously opens the pump (or starts it) if pressure has dropped below a threshold. The pump runs only when needed, reducing heat and improving efficiency. Pressurised systems have pressure regulators and accumulators to maintain a reserve of pressurised fluid. Most modern aircraft use pressurised systems because they're efficient and reliable. Open centre systems are lighter and simpler, so you'll find them on some older or very light aircraft.
exam_tip: Open centre: pump always runs, fluid always flowing, simple but inefficient. Pressurised: pump on demand, accumulator keeps pressure ready, efficient. Know which your aircraft uses and how to operate it.
key_facts: Open centre: pump always running, constant flow, high heat generation
Pressurised (closed centre): pump on demand, accumulators store pressure, efficient
Open centre: simpler, lighter, less maintenance
Pressurised: more complex, heavier, more efficient
Pressurised systems require regulators and accumulators
Modern aircraft typically use pressurised systems
Selector valve operation differs between system types
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the operation of a typical aircraft hydraulic system.
concept: A typical modern aircraft hydraulic system works like this: at engine start, the engine-driven pump begins pressurising hydraulic fluid from the reservoir. The pump pushes fluid through a pressure regulator (relief valve) set to, say, 2850 psi. If the system is idle, the regulator opens and excess flow returns to the reservoir, keeping pressure steady. If pressure drops below the set point, the regulator closes and the pump pressure rises to meet demand. Accumulators pre-charged with nitrogen to about 80% of system operating pressure sit in the lines, ready to supply power. When you command a function—say, gear down—the selector valve routes pressurised fluid into the landing gear actuator cylinder, pushing it. The gear descends, and the actuator displacement generates back-pressure, which the selector valve sees and routes return flow back to the reservoir through the filter. Throughout the system, check valves ensure fluid flows the right direction, and shutoff valves provide isolation for maintenance and safety. The fluid circulates through the filter to remove contaminants, then back to the reservoir. The whole cycle is continuous as long as you're demanding actuator movement. Once you release the selector, the actuator holds its position (locked by check valves), and the pump returns to low flow circulating mode.
exam_tip: Engine start: pump pressurises → regulator holds set pressure → selectors route flow on demand → actuators move → return flows through filter → reservoir. Accumulators provide reserve. Check valves hold actuator position.
key_facts: Engine-driven pump pressurises fluid from reservoir
Pressure regulator maintains system pressure at set point
Accumulators store pressurised fluid for backup and smoothing
Selector valve routes flow to desired actuator
Actuator extends/retracts under hydraulic pressure
Return flow passes through filter to remove contaminants
Check valves prevent backflow and hold actuator position
Shutoff valves provide isolation for safety and maintenance
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the relative: (a) advantages of pneumatic systems over hydraulic systems; (b) disadvantages of pneumatic systems over hydraulic systems.
concept: Pneumatic systems use compressed air or nitrogen instead of hydraulic fluid. The big advantage is safety—air is far less flammable than hydraulic oil, so you don't have the fire risk. They're also simpler, lighter, and cheaper because you're not dealing with complex pump and regulator systems. Servicing is easier; you just bleed off pressure and it's safe to work on. The downside is that air is compressible, so pneumatic systems are imprecise compared to hydraulics. You get pressure drop over time as the reservoir bleeds down, movement isn't as smooth, and you can't store as much energy in a small space. Hydraulics can generate enormous force from a compact system; pneumatics need bigger components for the same force output. Pneumatic systems are useful for light-duty applications: door actuation, cargo door locks, some landing gear on small aircraft. But for flight control surfaces or heavy landing gear, you need the precision and power of hydraulics. In NZ operations, you'll mostly see hydraulics on twins because they demand precision and reliability.
exam_tip: Pneumatic advantages: safe, simple, light. Disadvantages: compressible (imprecise), bulky, pressure drop, less force. Use on light-duty gear; hydraulics for critical systems.
key_facts: Pneumatic systems use compressed air or nitrogen
Advantages: safe (non-flammable), simple, light, inexpensive
Disadvantages: air is compressible, imprecise, pressure drop, bulky
Pneumatic unsuitable for precision flight control
Hydraulic preferred for landing gear and control surfaces
Pneumatic useful for doors, cargo latches, light actuators
Maintenance of pneumatic systems is simpler and safer
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the operation of a typical pneumatic system.
concept: A typical pneumatic system starts with a compressor (engine-driven or electric) that draws ambient air, pressurises it to 1000–3000 psi (depending on the system), and stores it in a pressure vessel (accumulator or tank). A pressure regulator maintains system pressure within operating limits. When a pneumatic actuator is commanded (via a solenoid valve or selector switch), pressurised air flows into the actuator, causing it to extend or retract. Return flow (or exhaust) is either recirculated or vented to atmosphere depending on the design. Moisture is a big issue in pneumatic systems because compressed air contains water vapour which can freeze in the lines or cause corrosion. So pneumatic systems have a dryer (desiccant cartridge) that removes moisture. On a light single or twin with pneumatic landing gear doors, the system pressure might be lower, around 1000 psi. Military and transport aircraft use higher pressures (2000–3000 psi) for more force in compact actuators. The system is inherently safe because there's no explosive fluid and the components tolerate higher pressure spikes without damage.
exam_tip: Compressor pressurises air → storage tank → regulator → solenoid/selector → actuator → exhaust. Dryer removes moisture. Simple and safe, but limited by air compressibility.
key_facts: Engine-driven or electric compressor pressurises air to 1000–3000 psi
Pressure vessel stores pressurised air
Regulator maintains system pressure
Solenoid valves (electric switches) direct flow to actuators
Pneumatic actuators extend on pressure, retract on exhaust
Dryer removes moisture to prevent freeze and corrosion
Return air vented to atmosphere or recirculated
Inherently safe; high pressure spikes don't risk rupture as with hydraulics
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the operation of a typical fire detection system.
concept: Fire detection systems use sensors (usually thermocouples or continuous-loop detectors) strategically placed in engine bays, cargo holds, or other high-risk areas. The most common in piston aircraft are continuous-loop systems: a Inconel or stainless-steel tube runs through the engine bay, and inside the tube is a eutectic wire (a specially formulated alloy) that melts at a fixed temperature—say, 180°C. When that temperature is reached, the wire melts, breaking the electrical circuit and triggering the fire warning on the flight deck. A thermocouple system uses multiple small thermocouples wired in series; if any thermocouple sees a rapid temperature rise (the rate-of-rise detector) or an absolute temperature above a threshold, it triggers the warning. Modern systems use both: absolute temperature (190°C) and rate-of-rise (15°C per second) so you catch a fire quickly. The warning activates a light (usually red, with 'FIRE' label) and possibly a bell or buzzer. It's not foolproof—false alarms do happen from electrical faults—but it's your first and often best warning that you've got a fire. Once the warning sounds, you isolate the affected engine and execute the fire-out procedure (cutting fuel, electrical power, closing firewall shutoff valves, deploying fire bottles if equipped).
exam_tip: Fire sensors: thermocouples or continuous-loop detectors in engine bay. Trigger on absolute temperature or rapid rate-of-rise. Warning light on flight deck. Know where your fire warning sensors are and what temperature triggers them.
key_facts: Continuous-loop systems: eutectic wire melts at set temperature
Thermocouple systems: detect absolute temperature or rate-of-rise
Sensors placed in engine bay, cargo hold, or other high-risk areas
Temperature threshold typically 180–200°C
Rate-of-rise detector: 10–15°C per second triggers warning
Warning activates red light and possibly audible alarm
False alarms can occur from electrical faults
Pilot action on warning: isolate engine, cut fuel, execute fire-out procedure
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain any precautions to be taken when using the common fire extinguishing agents available.
concept: Common aircraft fire extinguishing agents are CO2 (carbon dioxide), Halon 1211, Halon 1301, and dry powder (mostly on ground equipment). CO2 is effective but heavier than air, so it can stratify and leave pockets of oxygen-rich air. It's also cold, which can shock a hot engine. Halon 1211 and 1301 are halogenated hydrocarbons that interrupt the chemical reaction of combustion—they're extremely effective and leave no residue. Halon is the preferred agent on modern aircraft because of its efficiency. However, Halon production is being phased out worldwide due to ozone depletion concerns, so supplies are dwindling and very expensive. Dry powder is used in ground-based systems (hangars, maintenance areas) because it's messy but cheap and effective. Precautions when using any extinguishing agent: ensure good ventilation because CO2 displaces oxygen (asphyxiation risk in confined spaces), wear protective equipment (gloves, eye protection), and follow manufacturer procedures exactly. Don't mix agents. If you deploy a fire bottle in flight, expect reduced visibility (Halon dissipates, but can cloud the cabin briefly), and be aware that some agents cool the engine significantly and can affect handling. After deploying any agent, plan an immediate landing—the fire might be suppressed but you want inspection and maintenance before flying again.
exam_tip: CO2: effective but cold, heavy, asphyxiation risk. Halon: effective, leaves no residue, expensive, phasing out. Dry powder: messy, ground-based. Precautions: ventilate, PPE, follow procedures, plan landing after deployment.
key_facts: CO2: effective, but heavy and cold, asphyxiation risk in confined spaces
Halon 1211/1301: very effective, no residue, preferred for aircraft
Dry powder: messy, ground-based, used in hangars and shops
Never mix fire extinguishing agents
Ventilation essential when using CO2
Personal protective equipment required (gloves, eye protection)
Expect visibility degradation with airborne agents
Plan immediate landing after in-flight agent deployment
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe typical fire extinguishing systems.
concept: A typical in-flight fire extinguishing system on a light twin consists of one or more fire bottles (pressurised cylinders of Halon or CO2) mounted in an accessible but protected location, usually the fuselage or an engine bay bulkhead. The bottle is connected via a distribution line (usually a pipe or rigid tube) to the suspected fire location. A pushbutton on the flight deck activates the system: you press the button, a solenoid valve opens, and pressurised extinguishing agent flows down the pipe to disperse in the engine compartment. Some systems have multiple bottles with individual switches (one for each engine) so you can suppress fires on either engine independently. Manual override systems exist on some aircraft: if the electrical system fails, you can pull a mechanical lever or cable to open the valve manually. Ground-based systems in hangars are similar: a pull handle or push button releases the agent over a wide area. All fire extinguishing systems have inspection and maintenance requirements: hydrostatic testing of the bottle (every few years), checking the pressure gauge to ensure the bottle is charged, and verifying all valves and lines are clear. A discharged fire bottle is useless, so this is a critical pre-flight item. Once a bottle is deployed, it must be professionally refilled before the aircraft flies again.
exam_tip: Fire bottle: pressurised, solenoid-activated, distribution line to engine bay, pushbutton activation. Know where your bottle is, what agent it contains, and its pressure status. Discharged bottle = no-go.
key_facts: Fire bottle: pressurised cylinder of Halon or CO2, mounted in fuselage
Solenoid valve: electrically activated, releases agent on pushbutton
Distribution line: pipe routing agent to fire location
Multi-engine aircraft: separate bottle or selector for each engine
Manual override: mechanical lever or cable backup if electrical fails
Inspection: hydrostatic testing every 5 years, pressure check before flight
Discharged bottle: unserviceable, must be refilled by certified facility
Post-deployment: plan immediate landing, ground aircraft for inspection
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the associated limitations of typical fire extinguishing systems.
concept: Fire extinguishing systems are good, but they have real limitations. First, they only suppress the fire where the agent is directed; if the fire's in an area the distribution line doesn't reach, the agent won't help. Second, they work best on fuel fires or electrical fires; structural fires (where the airframe itself is burning) are harder to suppress and may reignite after the agent dissipates. Third, the agent cools the engine rapidly, and that thermal shock can damage components or cause subsequent failures. Fourth, once the agent is deployed, it's gone—you get one shot. If it doesn't work, you've got no backup. Some aircraft have multiple bottles to address this, but it costs weight and space. Fifth, the agent can obscure vision (CO2 fog, Halon vapour) making it harder to see instruments or navigate. Sixth, deployment doesn't address the root cause—if you've got an electrical short that's causing the fire, the agent suppresses it, but the short's still there waiting to reignite. That's why immediate landing and professional investigation are mandatory after any fire suppression. Finally, false alarms do happen from faulty detectors or electrical faults, and deploying the bottle unnecessarily uses up your one-shot protection.
exam_tip: Fire suppression limitations: limited coverage area, doesn't address root cause, one-shot only, thermal shock risk, vision degradation, possible reignition. Always land immediately after any fire warning, even if suppression deployed.
key_facts: Agent reaches only the area covered by distribution lines
Structural fires difficult to suppress and prone to reignition
Rapid cooling (thermal shock) risks engine damage
Single-shot deployment; no backup if first attempt fails
Halon/CO2 fog reduces visibility
Suppression treats symptom, not root cause
Electrical faults that cause fire still exist after suppression
False alarms from detector faults waste agent protection
Immediate landing and professional inspection mandatory after fire warning
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between anti-icing systems and de-icing systems.
concept: Anti-icing systems prevent ice from forming in the first place. You run them proactively before you expect ice: on approach to an icing layer or when flying through known icing. Once activated, they continuously heat the surfaces (wings, tail, windscreen, pitot tube, prop) so ice can't accrete. Thermal anti-icing systems (using hot bleed air from the engine) are common on turbocharged aircraft. The advantage is simplicity and full protection as long as they're running. The downside is continuous power draw and engine bleed air loss. De-icing systems allow ice to form initially, then remove it. Pneumatic de-icing boots are the classic example: rubber tubes on the leading edge of wings inflate and deflate, cracking off accreted ice. They work in pulses (inflate 3 seconds, deflate 2 seconds) to break the ice loose. De-icing is reactive—you notice ice forming, you activate the boots, and they cycle to shed the ice. The advantage is lower continuous power draw; you only operate when ice is present. The disadvantage is it's reactive, not preventive, and you'll accumulate some ice briefly before the boots work. Modern aircraft often have both systems or hybrid systems. For a CPL flying piston twins in NZ, you're usually anti-icing on the windscreen and pitot heat, and maybe propeller anti-icing. Serious icing protection (boots) is rarer on light twins. The key point: anti-ice is always on in the icing layer; de-ice is cyclic and reactive.
exam_tip: Anti-ice: prevent ice formation, always on in icing. De-ice: remove accreted ice, cyclic operation. Know which your aircraft has and when to activate.
key_facts: Anti-ice: continuous heating, prevents ice formation, proactive
De-ice: allows initial ice formation, removes it periodically, reactive
Thermal anti-ice: bleed air heated and distributed to surfaces
Pneumatic de-ice: rubber boots inflate/deflate to crack ice
Anti-ice: always on in icing; power draw continuous
De-ice: cyclic operation, lower average power draw
Hybrid systems combine both for comprehensive protection
Piston twins typically have windscreen anti-ice and pitot heat
source: NZ CAa AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the operation and the proper handling of the following ice protection systems: (a) mechanical; (b) fluid; (c) thermal.
concept: Mechanical systems (pneumatic boots) have rubber tubes bonded to the leading edges of wings, tail, and sometimes the fuselage. An air pump or engine-driven compressor pressurises air to inflate these tubes in cycles. A timer or solenoid valve controls the inflation sequence: tubes inflate (usually for 3 seconds), then deflate (2 seconds), then repeat. As the tubes inflate, they push outward, cracking the accreted ice and breaking it loose to stream aft. You activate the boots when you see ice forming; they cycle continuously once on. Fluid systems inject heated fluid (usually alcohol or glycol) into leading edges through small nozzles. Gravity and airflow distribute the fluid across the wing surface, preventing ice adhesion. It's continuous and very effective, but it's a consumable system—you carry a finite quantity of fluid, so on long flights in heavy icing, you might run out. Thermal systems use engine bleed air (hot air tapped from the engine compressor) routed through ducts to wing and tail leading edges, and then to sensors like the pitot tube and windscreen. The bleed air keeps surfaces warm enough to prevent ice accretion. It's effective and clean (no consumables), but it reduces engine performance slightly because you're robbing bleed air. Your POH will specify which systems your aircraft has and the proper activation sequence.
exam_tip: Mechanical (boots): activate when ice forms, cycles continuously. Fluid: continuous injection, consumable supply. Thermal (bleed air): continuous heat, always available. Know your aircraft system and activation procedure.
key_facts: Mechanical: pneumatic tubes inflate/deflate to crack ice
Mechanical activation: switch to 'on' when ice appears
Fluid systems: heated fluid injected through nozzles onto leading edge
Fluid systems: consumable supply, monitor quantity
Thermal: hot bleed air routed to leading edges
Thermal: continuous and effective, slight engine performance loss
All systems require timely activation as ice starts forming
Monitor outside air temperature and moisture for icing conditions
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe typical systems for: (a) ice detection; (b) windscreen heating; (c) rain clearance.
concept: Ice detection systems use sensors to warn you when ice is forming. The most common is a vibrating element or a liquid-crystal display that changes colour when ice contacts it. Some modern systems use electrical impedance measurement to sense ice accumulation. Once ice is detected, a light or warning comes on to prompt you to activate your anti-ice/de-ice systems. Windscreen heating is almost universal on aircraft flying in known icing: electrical heating mats (resistive wire bonded to the glass) warm the windscreen to 40–50°C, preventing ice adhesion. You control it with a switch, and it draws significant electrical power (usually 500–1000 watts per pane). Some windscreens are divided into sections so you can heat just the pilot's pane if power is limited. Rain clearance on modern aircraft uses wipers (electrically driven, wiping windscreen or side windows) and windscreen heating combined. The heat evaporates fine rain, and wipers handle heavier flow. On older aircraft, wipers are manually operated (pull a knob repeatedly) or absent. Propeller anti-ice is common on twin-engine aircraft: electrical heating element bonded to the prop hub, switched on before entering known icing. Flying into rain without wiper capability is uncomfortable but not dangerous; flying into ice without windscreen heating is a genuine visibility hazard.
exam_tip: Ice detection: vibrating element or impedance sensor, triggers warning light. Windscreen heat: electrical mats, pilot and co-pilot separately. Wipers: electric or manual, supplement heat. Know which systems your aircraft has.
key_facts: Ice detection: vibrating element or impedance sensor signals 'on' when ice detected
Windscreen heating: electrical resistive mats, 40–50°C, dual-pane control typical
Windscreen heat power: 500–1000 watts per pane
Wipers: electric or manual, used with windscreen heat for rain clearance
Pitot heat: electrical heating of pitot tube to prevent icing and airspeed error
Propeller anti-ice: electrical heating of prop hub, on before known icing
Static discharge wicks: prevent radio interference and arcing
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the performance reductions associated with: (a) ice accumulation; (b) operation of anti-ice and de-icing equipment.
concept: Ice accumulation on the airframe is severely detrimental. Even a thin layer of rough ice (rime ice is crystalline, glazed ice is smooth but heavier) disrupts the airflow over lifting surfaces. Wings lose lift, drag increases, stalling speed goes up, and handling gets sluggish. In extreme accumulation, you can lose 15–30% of available lift and pick up 40–50% more drag. The aircraft becomes heavier (ice weighs something), climbs poorly, and is at risk of stalling even at normal flying speeds. If you pick up ice on the prop, it becomes unbalanced, vibration increases, and prop efficiency drops. Landing with ice on the aircraft is risky: the approach is unstable, stalling speed's higher, and you need longer runway. Operating anti-ice and de-ice systems also costs performance: bleed air anti-icing reduces available engine power by 5–10% (varies by engine), so you'll climb slower and cruise slower. Windscreen heating and propeller heating draw electrical power, increasing alternator load and reducing available electrical capacity for other systems. Pneumatic de-ice boots add weight and drag (even when deflated, they're not perfectly smooth), so you'll notice slightly reduced cruise speed. In known icing, you accept these performance penalties to maintain safety—you can't afford to accumulate ice just to save a knot or two. The strategy is: avoid icing if possible, or if you must enter, activate protection early and land at the first opportunity.
exam_tip: Ice cost: 15–30% lift loss, 40–50% drag increase, heavier, higher stalling speed, poor climb. Anti-ice cost: 5–10% engine power loss. De-ice cost: weight and drag. Know your aircraft performance degradation in icing.
key_facts: Ice on wings: disrupts airflow, reduces lift, increases drag
Wing ice: can increase stalling speed by 5–10 knots
Prop ice: unbalance, vibration, efficiency loss
Fuselage ice: added weight, reduced climb
Bleed air anti-ice: 5–10% engine power loss
Electrical heating: draws alternator power, reduces electrical capacity
De-ice boots: add weight and drag even when deflated
Cumulative: ice + anti-ice operation can halve aircraft performance in icing
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the function and operation of: (a) static discharge wicks; (b) bonding strips.
concept: Static discharge wicks (often called 'static wicks' or 'statics') are small spring-loaded metal filaments attached to the trailing edge of wings and tail surfaces. As the aircraft flies through the air, the friction between air molecules and the airframe builds up a static electrical charge. This charge wants to discharge, and if it discharges through avionics or radio equipment, it can damage them or cause radio interference (crackling, noise, weak reception). Static wicks provide a low-resistance path for this charge to bleed off gradually into the atmosphere, preventing the buildup. They're small and add negligible weight, but they're essential on aircraft with modern avionics. Bonding strips (copper or aluminium strips with conductive connectors) electrically tie together different parts of the airframe. The goal is to ensure all metallic structures are at the same electrical potential, preventing dangerous voltage differences. If the fuselage and wing tanks are at different potentials, arcing can occur during refuelling (static discharge risk) or between structures during flight. Bonding is critical during refuelling: you ground the refuelling cart, the aircraft, and ensure fuel trucks are bonded to the airframe so any static builds on the aircraft flow safely to ground. Loose or corroded bonding connections can fail; that's why they're checked during maintenance.
exam_tip: Static wicks: bleed off static charge, trailing edge locations. Bonding: equalise electrical potential between structures, prevent arcing. Both essential for safety and avionics protection.
key_facts: Static discharge wicks: small metal filaments trailing edge surfaces
Wicks bleed static charge gradually, preventing buildup
Charge buildup without wicks: radio interference, avionics damage
Bonding strips: conductive paths between fuselage, wings, control surfaces
Bonding equalises electrical potential, prevents arcing
Refuelling: ground cart and aircraft, ensure bonding connected
Loose bonding: arcing risk, radio interference
Corrosion of bonding strips must be cleaned and maintained
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the function and principle of operation of the following instruments: (a) tachometers (centrifugal, drag cup, electrical and electronic types); (b) manifold pressure and boost gauges; (c) oil pressure gauges (direct reading, remote indicating and ratiometer types); (d) fuel pressure gauges; (e) vacuum gauges; (f) cylinder head and exhaust gas temperature gauges (thermocouples); (g) oil temperature gauges; (h) outside air temperature gauges; (i) fuel quantity gauges; (j) fuel flow gauges.
concept: Your engine instruments are your window into what's happening inside the powerplants. A tachometer (rev counter) reads RPM: centrifugal types use weighted balls that fling outward as speed increases, pulling a needle; drag cup types use a spinning magnet creating drag proportional to RPM; electrical types use an alternator on the engine generating frequency proportional to RPM; electronic ones count pulses from the magnetos. Manifold pressure gauge shows intake air pressure in the intake manifold—at full throttle sea level you might see 30 inHg, at altitude it drops. Boost gauge is similar but measures turbocharger output in psi, warning you if boost's excessive (over-pressure risk). Oil pressure gauge (direct reading uses a Bourdon tube connected by a small pipe to engine oil; remote indicating uses a transducer on the engine sending an electrical signal to the gauge; ratiometer compares two resistances to indicate pressure on a moving needle). Fuel pressure gauge tells you fuel pump output—normal range is 4–6 psi on carburetted engines, higher on fuel-injected ones. Vacuum gauge (or suction gauge) measures negative pressure created by the vacuum pump—typical range is 4–5.5 inHg—and tells you whether your gyro-powered instruments are getting good suction. Cylinder head temperature (CHT) and exhaust gas temperature (EGT) use thermocouples (two dissimilar metals creating a tiny voltage in response to heat); the gauge measures this voltage and converts it to temperature. CHT typically 350–400°F max; EGT varies by engine but tells you if a cylinder's running lean or rich. Oil temperature gauge (similar thermocouple sensor in the oil sump) shows oil heat—normal range is 165–220°F. Outside air temperature gauge (OAT) uses a small Bourdon tube that expands/contracts with air temperature, or an electronic thermistor—gives you true air temperature for calculations and icing awareness. Fuel quantity gauge uses a float-operated rheostat in the tank (like a car fuel gauge) sending resistance to the gauge, which converts resistance to fuel quantity. Fuel flow gauge shows fuel consumption rate (pounds per hour or gallons per hour) based on a turbine or gear pump in the fuel line that spins faster with higher flow, generating frequency that the gauge displays.
exam_tip: Know each instrument's principle and what normal values look like. Understand which are direct-reading (oil pressure Bourdon tube) versus remote-indicating (fuel quantity). Thermocouple-based gauges (CHT, EGT, OAT) measure voltage from heat.
key_facts: Tachometer: centrifugal, drag cup, electrical, or electronic; measures RPM
Manifold pressure: intake air pressure, normal 30 inHg at sea level full throttle
Boost gauge: turbocharger outlet pressure, measures in psi
Oil pressure: direct reading (Bourdon tube), remote (transducer), ratiometer
Fuel pressure: 4–6 psi carburetted, higher fuel-injected
Vacuum/suction: 4–5.5 inHg required for gyro instruments
CHT (thermocouple): cylinder head temp, max typically 400°F
EGT (thermocouple): exhaust gas temp, indicates lean/rich condition
Oil temperature (thermocouple): 165–220°F normal range
OAT: outside air temperature, Bourdon or thermistor
Fuel quantity: float rheostat in tank, converts to gauge reading
Fuel flow: turbine or gear sensor measures consumption rate (pph/gph)
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the principle of operation of a simple AC synchro system.
concept: A synchro system synchronises the position of two instruments: a transmitter at the engine-driven source (say, a tachometer sensor on the engine) and a receiver on the flight deck (the tachometer gauge). Here's how it works: the transmitter is a small AC generator whose output voltage phase shifts as the source (engine RPM) changes. This variable-voltage AC signal travels through wires to the receiver, which is a motor with a rotor and stator. The incoming AC signal drives the rotor to a specific angle that corresponds to the input signal. As engine RPM changes, the transmitter signal changes, and the receiver rotor moves in sync, dragging the needle with it. It's electrical synchronisation—the receiver always knows where the transmitter is. Synchro systems are used on fuel quantity gauges (float position in tank drives a synchro transmitter), engine temperature gauges, and some fuel flow systems. The advantage is no direct mechanical linkage is needed, so the sensor can be anywhere on the engine and the gauge anywhere on the flight deck. The disadvantage is if the AC power (usually 400 Hz on large aircraft, variable on small ones) is lost, the gauge becomes unreliable or sticks. On a piston twin with basic instrumentation, you might have direct-reading gauges (Bourdon tube oil pressure, float-type fuel quantity) rather than synchro systems, keeping it simple. Synchro systems are more elegant and remote but require intact electrical and AC power systems.
exam_tip: Synchro transmitter: AC generator at source, voltage phase-shifts with input. Synchro receiver: AC motor on gauge, rotor angle matches transmitter. System loses accuracy if AC power fails.
key_facts: Synchro transmitter: AC generator at engine, output phase varies with parameter (RPM, fuel level)
Synchro receiver: AC motor on gauge, rotor angle follows transmitter
AC signal transmitted through wires, no mechanical linkage
Receiver needle moves in sync with transmitter input
AC frequency critical: 400 Hz on large aircraft, varies on small ones
Fault: AC power loss causes gauge unreliability or sticking
Used for fuel quantity, CHT, EGT on modern aircraft
Direct-reading gauges (Bourdon, float) alternative on simple systems
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Define: (a) static air pressure; (b) dynamic air pressure; (c) total (pitot) pressure.
concept: Static air pressure is the pressure of the air around you at rest—it's the ambient pressure at your altitude. It decreases as you climb (lower density, less pressure). Dynamic air pressure is the pressure created by the motion of the aircraft through the air—it's zero if you're stationary and increases with airspeed.
