The TC is better for instrument flying because it gives you information about both turning and rolling, helping you fly coordinated manoeuvres. A standard rate turn on a TC still aligns the needle with the standard rate mark. Many modern aircraft now have electronic display systems instead of mechanical TC instruments, but the principle is the same. The TC is more complete for instrument flying: it tells you the aircraft's attitude (pitch/roll) implicitly and the turn rate explicitly, whereas the TI only tells you turn rate.
exam_tip: TI: yaw only, doesn't show roll or bank immediately. TC: yaw and roll, needle moves on bank. TC better for instrument flying. Standard rate same for both: needle at mark = 3°/sec.
key_facts: Turn indicator (TI): senses yaw only, gyro horizontal left-right
TI: needle moves only when aircraft yaws
Turn co-ordinator (TC): senses yaw and roll, gyro tilted 30–45°
TC: needle moves on bank and yaw
TC better instrument-flying reference: shows attitude and rate
TI standard rate: 3°/sec, needle at mark
TC standard rate: same, 3°/sec at mark
Modern versions: electronic display, not mechanical gyro
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the errors which a turn indicator is subject to.
concept: A turn indicator responds to yaw rate only, so it's immune to pitch and roll changes (assuming no yaw component). The main error is instrument lag: when you first yaw, the rotor needs a moment to precess, and the needle takes a second or two to respond. In an aggressive turn entry, the needle might lag the actual turn rate by several seconds. This lag is minor and typically accepted. The second source of error is compass card or dial lag: once the gyro rotor detects yaw and precesses, the mechanical indicator (needle and dial) has inertia and can overshoot or oscillate before settling. This is rarely significant in practice. A third source is precession caused by aircraft acceleration or deceleration that creates forces on the gyro rotor apart from yaw. If the aircraft accelerates forward, inertia pushes the gyro backward, causing unwanted precession and a false turn indication. Similarly, a sudden altitude change or climb/descent can cause the rotor to precess if the gimbal friction isn't perfect. These effects are usually small and brief. The main practical error is understanding that the TI doesn't sense roll: in a coordinated turn, the TI needle will show turn rate, but the needle position doesn't tell you bank angle. You need the attitude indicator for bank information. That's why modern aircraft have turn co-ordinators instead—they sense both yaw and roll. In an old TI-only equipped aircraft, you cross-check with visual reference or attitude estimation.
exam_tip: TI errors: lag (few seconds), doesn't sense roll (need attitude reference), acceleration effects (minor). Understand it's yaw-only; cross-check with visual or attitude indicator.
key_facts: Lag: rotor precession delay, 1–2 seconds typical
Dial overshoot: mechanical inertia, rare in practice
Acceleration effects: forward accel can cause false turn indication
Roll insensitivity: doesn't sense banking directly
No bank angle info: need attitude indicator for bank
Altitude changes: can cause minor precession if gimbal friction high
Practical: accept lag, cross-check with attitude and visual reference
Modern aircraft: turn co-ordinator eliminates roll error
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the principle of operation of the balance ball.
concept: The balance ball (slip/skid indicator) is a simple mechanical device that shows whether your turn is coordinated. It's a ball rolling inside a curved glass tube mounted on the turn indicator or turn co-ordinator instrument. When the aircraft is in a coordinated turn, the ball centres in the tube. When the aircraft is skidding (turning too fast for the bank angle—turning too hard for the bank you've got), centrifugal force pushes the ball outward (away from the turn direction). When the aircraft is slipping (turning too slowly for the bank—banking harder than needed for the turn rate), the ball rolls inward (toward the turn direction). The pilot's job in a coordinated turn is to keep the ball centred, usually using rudder input. The rudder controls yaw; by adjusting rudder, you change the turn rate, which changes the balance of centrifugal force and gravity/lift, moving the ball until it centres. It's a feedback mechanism: if the ball is left of centre in a right turn, you're slipping, so you add right rudder to increase turn rate. If it's right of centre, you're skidding, so you add left rudder to decrease turn rate. The balance ball is simple, requires no power, and is immediately obvious to the pilot. In instrument flight, a coordinated turn keeps the aircraft stable and reduces stress on the airframe. The phrase 'needle, ball, airspeed' is a classic instrument scan for basic flying: check the turn indicator needle, check the ball for coordination, and check airspeed. The ball is part of every turn indicator and turn co-ordinator.
exam_tip: Balance ball: centred = coordinated. Left of centre in right turn = slipping (add inside rudder). Right of centre in right turn = skidding (add outside rudder). Simple, reliable, no power needed.
key_facts: Balance ball: ball in curved tube, no power required
Centred: turn is coordinated
Ball left (toward centre): slipping, add inside rudder
Ball right (toward outside): skidding, add outside rudder
Responds to: imbalance between centrifugal and gravitational forces
Used in: turn indicator, turn co-ordinator
Pilot technique: 'step on the ball' (add rudder in direction ball rolls)
Essential for: instrument flying, coordinated manoeuvres
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Interpret various TI and TC indications.
concept: Turn indicator indications are straightforward: needle pointing left = turning left, needle pointing right = turning right. The further the needle deflects, the faster the turn rate. The needle aligning with the standard rate mark (usually marked on the dial) indicates a standard rate turn of 3 degrees per second. On the ground, the needle should be centred (no turn). In flight during a level straight-and-level segment, the needle should be centred. During a turn, the needle deflects proportionally to yaw rate. Turn co-ordinator indications include both needle position (turn rate) and a small aircraft symbol: the symbol shows bank angle (tilts left or right), and the needle shows turn rate. An aircraft symbol level with wings horizontal indicates level flight. Tilted left or right indicates banking in that direction. The needle deflection superimposed on the aircraft symbol tells you both attitude and rate. Interpreting the ball: ball centred = coordinated, ball left = slipping (need inside rudder), ball right = skidding (need outside rudder). A practical scan on instruments: check the turn indicator/co-ordinator needle and ball together. In a level turn, the needle should show appropriate turn rate (say, 1/3 deflection for a shallow turn, full standard rate mark for a steeper turn) and the ball should be centred. If the ball is off-centre, adjust rudder to centre it while maintaining the desired turn rate. On a TC, also check the aircraft symbol for bank angle: is it tilted appropriately for the turn rate? If turn rate is high but bank is shallow, the aircraft is slipping and losing altitude.
exam_tip: TI needle: left = left turn, right = right turn. Standard rate = mark. Ball: centred = good. TC: aircraft symbol shows bank, needle shows rate. Scan both together.
key_facts: Needle deflection: proportional to yaw rate
Standard rate mark: TI/TC needle aligns when 3°/sec turn
Ground/level flight: needle centred
Ball centred: coordinated
Ball displaced: slipping or skidding, adjust rudder
TC aircraft symbol: level = level flight, tilted = banked
TC symbol tilt: indicates bank angle
Scan: needle, ball, airspeed, attitude together
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the serviceability checks for the TI/TC.
concept: On the ground before flight, the turn indicator or turn co-ordinator needle should be centred (no turn). If it's deflected, the instrument might have a mechanical sticking problem or the gyro might be spinning slower than normal. The vacuum gauge should read 4–5.5 inHg; if lower, the gyro spins slower and the instrument is sluggish or unreliable. After starting the engine and allowing a minute or two for vacuum to build, the needle should still be centred (once vacuum is stabilised). The balance ball should also be centred. During flight, you monitor the instrument: when you turn, the needle should respond immediately and smoothly. A sluggish or delayed response suggests low vacuum or mechanical friction. After completing a turn and rolling level, the needle should return to centre promptly (within a second or two). If it's slow or sticks, suspect vacuum loss or mechanical binding. The ball should move smoothly and return to centre when you centre the rudder. An erratic or sticking ball indicates mechanical damage. If you notice the instrument consistently reads off (e.g., needle slightly deflected during level flight), it might be a mechanical problem with the needle bearing. Modern electronic turn co-ordinators (which use an electronic rate sensor instead of a mechanical gyro) should power up and display a level aircraft symbol with a centred needle. If not, suspect an electrical or sensor fault. Any serviceability issue (sluggish, sticking, erratic, won't centre) means the instrument is unserviceable and must be repaired before the next flight.
exam_tip: Pre-flight: needle centred, ball centred, vacuum 4–5.5 inHg. In-flight: needle responds promptly, ball centred in coordinated flight. Sluggish or sticking = unserviceable.
key_facts: Ground check: needle centred, ball centred, vacuum 4–5.5 inHg
After engine start: vacuum builds, needle should remain centred
In-flight: prompt needle response to yaw, smooth motion
Turn completion: needle returns to centre quickly (1–2 sec)
Ball: smooth movement, centres when turn is coordinated
Sluggish response: low vacuum or mechanical friction
Sticking needle or ball: mechanical binding, unserviceable
Electronic TC: checks electrical power, sensor function
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the principle of operation of a direction indicator/heading indicator (DI/HI).
concept: A direction indicator (or heading indicator, DI/HI) is a gyroscopic instrument that displays the aircraft's magnetic heading. Unlike a magnetic compass, the DI doesn't respond to Earth's magnetic field directly; instead, it uses a vertical-axis gyroscope to maintain a fixed heading reference. The gyro rotor spins around the vertical axis (up-down). Because of rigidity in space, the rotor maintains its orientation relative to inertial space. A heading card (disk) is geared to the rotor; as the gyro maintains its orientation, the card maintains a fixed heading display. The pilot aligns the heading card to the magnetic compass heading on the ground before flight (using a knob to adjust the card). Once set, the card doesn't change unless you manually adjust the knob or the gyro precesses (drifts). In flight, the DI displays the set heading even though no external reference is being sensed. This is useful because the DI has no lag (unlike the magnetic compass with its turning errors and acceleration errors) and it's smooth and stable. The disadvantage is the gyro drifts over time due to friction and mechanical imperfections in the gimbals (real drift or real wander), and it can topple if tilted excessively (in a steep dive or climb with unusual attitude, gimbal limits can be exceeded and the gyro flips). To maintain accuracy, you periodically synchronise the DI to the magnetic compass: you glance at the compass, note the difference, and reset the DI card using the knob. Standard practice is to reset every 10–15 minutes in flight.
exam_tip: DI/HI: vertical-axis gyro maintains heading. Rigidity keeps card fixed. Set to compass before flight. Drift requires periodic reset to compass. No lag or compass errors; much smoother for instrument flying.
key_facts: DI/HI: vertical-axis gyroscope, maintains magnetic heading
Rigidity in space: card maintains set heading independent of turns
Heading card: geared to gyro rotor, displays magnetic heading
Set-up: synchronise card to magnetic compass before flight
Adjustment knob: manual synchronisation to compass in flight
Advantages: no lag, no turning errors, smooth and stable
Disadvantages: drifts over time, needs periodic reset
Reset frequency: every 10–15 minutes during flight
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the following errors which the DI/HI is subject to; (a) gimbal error; (b) real drift (or real wander); (c) apparent drift (or apparent wander); (d) low rotor speed.
concept: Gimbal error occurs when the gyro rotor axis tilts beyond its design limit, causing the gimbals to misalign. If you fly a very steep dive or climb or an unusual attitude, the rotor's physical supports (gimbals) hit their mechanical stops, and the rotor loses its alignment with the vertical axis. When you recover to normal flight, the rotor has toppled and the DI card now shows incorrect heading. This is a catastrophic error in the sense that the DI is now unreliable until reset to the compass. Real drift (or real wander) is gradual precession of the rotor due to friction in the gimbal bearings and other mechanical imperfections. Over time (typically 15–20 minutes depending on gyro quality), the rotor slowly precesses and the heading card drifts. This is why periodic reset to the compass is necessary. Apparent drift (or apparent wander) is drift that appears to occur but is actually caused by the aircraft's latitude change. As you fly north or south, the rate at which magnetic heading changes (due to Earth's curvature and variation change) differs from how your gyro perceives heading. At high latitudes, this effect is noticeable. Low rotor speed (below design RPM) causes reduced rigidity and increased drift. If vacuum pressure drops below 4.5 inHg, the rotor slows, and the DI becomes sluggish and drifts faster. Very low vacuum (near zero) makes the gyro unreliable. A 15-minute reset schedule is the standard practice to manage real drift. In practice, gimbal error is rare if you fly within normal operating limits. Real and apparent drift are managed by periodic synchronisation to the magnetic compass.
exam_tip: Gimbal error: steep attitude breaks gimbal limits, topples rotor. Real drift: friction causes slow precession, reset every 15 min. Apparent drift: latitude effect, managed by reset. Low vacuum: slows rotor, increases drift.
key_facts: Gimbal error: exceeding gimbal limits topples rotor (unusual attitudes)
Rotor topple: card shows incorrect heading after recovery
Real drift: friction in gimbals, slow precession over time
Real drift rate: 1–3°/minute typical (varies by quality)
Apparent drift: latitude-related magnetic heading change
Reset frequency: 15-minute standard schedule
Low rotor speed: reduces rigidity, increases drift rate
Low vacuum: <4.5 inHg, rotor slows, DI unreliable
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the characteristics of a toppled directional gyro (DI/HI).
concept: A toppled DI rotor has lost its alignment with the vertical axis. The rotor is tilted, and the heading card is no longer level. Characteristics of a toppled DI include: the heading card will show erratic or nonsensical headings (it might show multiple headings simultaneously if viewed from different angles), the card might be tilted or off-level, and most obviously, the card will show incorrect heading compared to the magnetic compass. If you topple the DI in a dive (by exceeding gimbal limits), you might recover from the dive and roll level, but the DI card will be tilted and showing wrong heading. You can't rely on the reading. To verify topple, compare the DI heading to your magnetic compass: if they differ by more than a small amount (say, >10 degrees) and the DI card looks tilted, the DI is toppled. Recovery: you can't fix a toppled DI in flight—the gimbal is mechanically locked. You must land, shut down, restart (allowing the rotor to spin up again), and re-synchronise to the compass. Some DIs have a 'cage' knob (a mechanical lock) that locks the rotor in the vertical position for ground use only; caging the DI on the ground after a topple helps re-align it before the next flight. Flying with a toppled DI is dangerous because you're flying blind on heading—you have only the magnetic compass for reference, and the magnetic compass has turning errors and lag. The lesson is to avoid unusual attitudes that exceed gimbal limits.
exam_tip: Toppled DI: rotor tilted, card shows wrong heading, may appear askew. Compare to compass to verify. Can't fix in flight. Land and reset. Avoid unusual attitudes to prevent topple.
key_facts: Toppled DI: rotor has lost vertical alignment
Heading card: shows incorrect heading, may appear tilted
Erratic reading: card might show multiple headings if tilted
Verification: compare DI to magnetic compass; large difference = topple
Gimbal lock: mechanical stops prevent recovery in flight
Fix: land, restart gyro, synchronise to compass
Cage function: locks rotor vertically for ground recovery
Prevention: avoid unusual attitudes exceeding gimbal limits
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the DI/HI typical: (a) operating limitations; (b) serviceability checks.
concept: Operating limitations of a DI: the DI should only be used for heading reference during normal aircraft attitudes. Extreme pitch or roll (dives, loops, unusual attitudes) can cause gimbal lock and toppling. The DI should not be relied upon for heading reference beyond 15 minutes without synchronisation to the magnetic compass. Vacuum must be maintained at 4–5.5 inHg; low vacuum causes the rotor to slow and drift increases. The DI is not suitable for navigation at extreme latitudes (above 70–75 degrees) where apparent drift becomes significant. Serviceability checks on the ground: the DI heading card should be set to match the magnetic compass heading using the adjustment knob. The card should rotate smoothly when you adjust the knob, and the compass rose should rotate without sticking. Vacuum gauge should read 4–5.5 inHg. After engine start and allowing vacuum to stabilise (a minute or so), the heading card should still be aligned with the compass. In flight: periodically (every 10–15 minutes), check the DI against the magnetic compass and reset if drift is noticeable (more than a few degrees). Monitor vacuum; if it drops below 4.5 inHg, the DI becomes unreliable. If the DI shows sudden heading change or toppling is suspected, compare to compass and visual reference. If disagreement persists, the DI is unserviceable and you must rely on magnetic compass and visual heading reference. A DI that's drifting faster than normal (more than 5 degrees per 10 minutes) indicates gimbal bearing wear and needs overhaul.
exam_tip: Limitations: normal attitudes only, 15-min sync, 4–5.5 inHg vacuum needed. Checks: set to compass pre-flight, monitor drift, reset periodic. Fast drift = wear.
key_facts: Operating limit: normal aircraft attitudes, avoid extremes
Synchronisation: reset to compass every 10–15 minutes
Vacuum requirement: 4–5.5 inHg for stable operation
Low vacuum: <4.5 inHg causes unreliable DI
Latitude limit: ~70° where apparent drift significant
Ground check: set to compass, knob rotates smoothly
In-flight: periodic sync to compass, monitor vacuum
Drift rate: >5°/10 min indicates mechanical wear
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the advantages of an electrically-driven DI/HI versus an air-driven instrument.
concept: An air-driven (vacuum-driven) DI depends on the vacuum pump operating reliably. If the pump fails, the rotor slows, and the DI becomes unreliable or fails completely. Also, the vacuum system is complex (pump, regulator, filter, relief valve, gauges) and needs maintenance. An electrically-driven DI uses an electric motor to spin the rotor. It requires electrical power (AC or DC depending on design) but doesn't depend on an engine-driven vacuum pump. If the alternator fails but the battery is good, the electric DI might continue working longer than a vacuum-driven one. Electric drive is also more compact and can be designed with lower power draw. The main advantage is redundancy: if one gyro system fails, you still have the other. On modern glass-panel aircraft, the DI is often replaced by an electronic heading display (part of the attitude/heading reference system, AHRS) that uses heading sensors and no mechanical gyro at all. The disadvantages of electric drive: it depends on electrical power, and some electric DIs draw significant current (reducing available electrical capacity). If both the alternator and battery fail, the electric DI fails too. Vacuum-driven systems are mechanical and don't have electrical dependencies, but they're heavier and bulkier. Most modern aircraft transitioning away from mechanical gyro instruments favour electronic displays that use solid-state sensors (magnetometers, accelerometers) instead, providing both advantages: no mechanical complexity and no vacuum/electrical dependencies for the core heading reference.
exam_tip: Electric DI: independent of vacuum, depends on electrical power, compact. Vacuum DI: independent of power, depends on pump. Modern: electronic AHRS, no mechanical gyro needed.
key_facts: Vacuum-driven: depends on engine-driven pump, complex system
Vacuum advantage: independent of electrical power
Vacuum disadvantage: pump failure = DI failure
Electric-driven: uses electric motor to spin rotor
Electric advantage: independent of vacuum system
Electric disadvantage: depends on electrical power supply
Electronic (AHRS): solid-state sensors, no mechanical gyro
Electronic advantage: small, robust, fast response, no maintenance
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the principle of operation of an artificial horizon/attitude indicator (AH/AI).
concept: An artificial horizon (attitude indicator, AH/AI) is a gyroscopic instrument that displays the aircraft's pitch and roll attitude. A gyro rotor spins around a horizontal fore-aft axis (parallel to the fuselage). Due to rigidity in space, the rotor maintains a level orientation regardless of how the aircraft moves. A mechanical linkage transmits the rotor's orientation to an attitude indicator display: a ball or model airplane that tilts with the rotor, showing pitch (nose up or down) and roll (wings tilted). The display is set against a horizon line: the ball's upper hemisphere (sky) is dark or blue, the lower hemisphere (ground) is tan or brown. Pitch is indicated by the position of the model aircraft relative to the horizon line: nose above = climbing, nose below = descending. Roll is indicated by the tilt of the model: wings level = 0 degrees bank, tilted left = left bank, tilted right = right bank. The artificial horizon is vacuum-powered (or electrically-powered in modern variants). A vacuum-powered AH is typically the most reliable instrument because the rotor is spinning continuously and maintains its level orientation without requiring external reference. In instrument flight, the artificial horizon is your primary pitch and roll reference—it tells you immediately whether you're climbing, descending, or banking, which is critical for safe instrument flying. The AH doesn't drift like a directional indicator because level (horizontal) is an absolute reference—gravity defines what's level. Modern electronic displays (glass cockpits) replace the mechanical AH with a digital display fed by an AHRS (attitude heading reference system) using solid-state sensors, but the principle is the same: display aircraft pitch and roll attitude.
exam_tip: AH/AI: horizontal fore-aft gyro maintains level. Display shows pitch and roll relative to horizon line. Primary instrument for attitude in instrument flight. Vacuum or electric powered.
key_facts: Artificial horizon (AH): horizontal fore-aft gyro spins
Rigidity: rotor maintains level orientation in inertial space
Display: attitude ball or model aircraft on horizon line
Pitch: ball position relative to horizon (above = climb, below = descent)
Roll: ball tilt relative to horizon (tilted = banked)
Vacuum-powered: engine-driven pump, continuous operation
Electrically-powered: motor drive, similar operation
Modern: electronic AHRS provides same indication digitally
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the effect on an air-driven AH/AI, including the errors in indication arising from a: (a) straight-line acceleration; (b) turn.
concept: A straight-line acceleration (climbing while accelerating) can cause errors in the AH display. Inertia from the acceleration creates a force that tilts the gyro rotor off its level axis. In a climb with forward acceleration, the forward inertia pushes the gyro rotor backward, tilting it, and the AH display shows a pitch-up error (shows more climb than actual). The error fades as the acceleration ends and the rotor settles back to level. In level flight with acceleration, the AH might show a slight climb indication momentarily. In a descent with deceleration (pulling back while descending), the deceleration force pushes the rotor forward, creating a pitch-down error (shows more descent). During a turn, the gyro rotor experiences a centrifugal-like effect due to Earth's rotation and the aircraft's circular motion. In a coordinated turn at constant altitude and constant turn rate, the AH should show constant bank angle and level pitch. However, the AH has limitations: if the turn is very steep or the aircraft is uncoordinated (slipping or skidding), the AH can show erratic pitch (false climb or descent indications). In a steep turn with rapid roll-in, the AH might momentarily show a pitch change even though the aircraft isn't actually pitching. These errors are usually small and temporary, settling out as the manoeuvre stabilises. The errors are worst in rapid manoeuvres or extreme attitudes. For normal flying, the AH is accurate enough to rely upon. The error is also why cross-checking with airspeed and altitude instruments is important: if the AH shows climb but altitude isn't increasing and airspeed is stable, something's wrong (possible AH error or unusual aircraft response).
exam_tip: Acceleration: AH shows erratic pitch during accel/decel, settles after. Turns: AH shows constant bank, but steep/uncoordinated turns can cause pitch errors. Cross-check with airspeed and altitude.
key_facts: Acceleration error: forward accel tilts rotor, shows pitch-up
Deceleration error: backward accel tilts rotor, shows pitch-down
Error magnitude: usually small (5–10° or less)
Error duration: temporary, settles as manoeuvre ends
Turn at constant altitude: AH shows correct constant bank
Steep/rapid turns: erratic pitch indication possible
Uncoordinated flight: slip/skid causes pitch errors
Cross-check: airspeed and altitude should match AH indication
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the general operating limitations of an: (a) air-driven AH/AI; (b) electrically-driven AH/AI.
concept: Air-driven (vacuum-driven) AH/AI limitations: requires vacuum at 4–5.5 inHg to operate reliably. If vacuum drops below 4.5 inHg, the rotor spins slower, rigidity decreases, and the instrument becomes unreliable. Complete vacuum loss (pump failure) causes the rotor to stop spinning and the AH to tumble and show incorrect attitude. The vacuum system is engine-dependent: if the engine fails, the vacuum pump stops, and the AH fails. Air-driven AH/AI can display a "cage" or "erect" mechanism (a manual knob) that locks the rotor level for ground use and initial spin-up. Electrically-driven AH/AI limitations: requires electrical power (AC or DC). Loss of electrical power means the rotor stops and the AH fails. Some electric AH/AI instruments draw significant current, reducing electrical capacity available for other systems. Electric drive is sometimes used with a standby battery so the AH continues to work briefly after main power loss, providing time to transition to manual flying or compass navigation. Modern integrated systems (glass cockpits with AHRS) depend on electrical power and correct sensor operation. Both air-driven and electric-driven AH/AI have operating limits on aircraft attitude: they're designed for normal flight (up to roughly ±30 degrees of pitch and roll). Extremely unusual attitudes (inverted, vertical dives) can cause gimbal lock or toppling, and the AH would show incorrect attitude until recovery to normal flight. For both types, periodic alignment or synchronisation is not required (unlike the DI) because level is an absolute reference defined by gravity.
exam_tip: Air-driven: needs vacuum 4–5.5 inHg, engine-dependent, pump failure = failure. Electric: needs power, power loss = failure. Both: normal attitudes only (<±30° pitch/roll), gimbal limits at extremes.
key_facts: Air-driven AH: vacuum-powered, requires 4–5.5 inHg
Low vacuum: rotor slows, AH becomes unreliable
Vacuum loss: rotor stops, AH tumbles and fails
Engine dependence: engine failure = pump failure = AH failure
Electric AH: motor-driven, requires electrical power
Power loss: rotor stops, AH fails immediately
Standby power: some electric AH have battery backup
Attitude limits: designed for normal flight, extreme attitudes cause errors
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Distinguish between the indications of the TC and AH/AI under various conditions.
concept: Turn co-ordinator (TC) shows both turn rate and bank angle (via the tilted aircraft symbol). The needle indicates rate of turn (standard rate at the mark). The aircraft symbol shows bank angle (tilted left/right for bank, level for level flight). The balance ball shows coordination. Artificial horizon (AH/AI) shows pitch and roll attitude directly. The horizon line stays fixed; the aircraft symbol pitches up/down and rolls left/right relative to the horizon. No turn-rate indication (the AH doesn't explicitly show how fast you're turning). Under level flight: TC shows level aircraft symbol, centred needle, centred ball. AH shows aircraft level with horizon line, level pitch. Under a turn: TC shows tilted aircraft symbol (banking) and deflected needle (turn rate). Ball shows coordination. AH shows rolled aircraft (tilted wings relative to horizon) and possibly pitched (depending on climb/descent during turn). Under a climb: TC shows the aircraft symbol slightly tilted and a slight needle deflection (if there's any yaw component of the climb). AH shows nose-up pitch, wings level if it's a straight climb. Under a descent: TC similar to climb (needle might show slight deflection if yawed). AH shows nose-down pitch. Under a turn while climbing: TC shows both bank and turn rate, ball coordination. AH shows both roll (wings tilted) and pitch (nose up). Distinguishing: TC is primarily for turn rate and bank; AH is primarily for pitch and roll attitude. In instrument flying, you use both together: TC tells you turn rate and helps coordinate, AH tells you pitch and roll attitude. A classic instrument cross-check uses all three (needle, ball, airspeed) plus AH for a complete picture: TC needle = turn rate, ball = coordination, AH = attitude, airspeed = speed being maintained. Together they define the aircraft's flight path and motion.
exam_tip: TC: turn rate (needle) and bank (symbol). AH: pitch and roll. Use together: TC for rate/coordination, AH for attitude. Both needed for complete instrument flying picture.
key_facts: TC needle: turn rate, standard rate at mark
TC aircraft symbol: bank angle (tilted = banked)
TC ball: coordination (centred = coordinated)
AH: pitch attitude (nose up/down relative to horizon)
AH: roll attitude (wing tilt relative to horizon)
TC no pitch indication, AH no explicit turn-rate indication
Level flight: TC level symbol centred needle, AH level attitude
Climbing turn: TC shows bank and turn, AH shows pitch and roll
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the principle of a Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS).
concept: A GNSS (like GPS, GLONASS, Galileo) works by calculating your position based on signals from satellites orbiting Earth. Each satellite broadcasts a radio signal containing its location and the precise time the signal was transmitted. Your receiver picks up signals from at least four satellites (more is better for accuracy). By measuring the time delay between transmission and reception, the receiver calculates the distance to each satellite (distance = speed of light × time delay). With distances to four or more satellites, geometry allows the receiver to pinpoint your exact position (latitude, longitude, altitude). The principle is trilateration: you need at least three satellites to find a 2D position (latitude and longitude), and a fourth for altitude. GPS (USA) has about 30 satellites in orbit; Galileo (EU) has similar coverage; GLONASS (Russia) and BeiDou (China) provide backup. In practice, more satellites mean better accuracy and faster position updates. Your aircraft GNSS receiver calculates position continuously (typically 1–10 times per second depending on the unit) and displays your location on a moving map or alongside your compass heading. Modern glass cockpits integrate GNSS position with other sensors (air data computer, heading reference) to provide a complete picture: your location, altitude, heading, and velocity. GNSS is incredibly accurate (within metres for civilian receivers, centimetres for military or specialized receivers) and is unaffected by weather. It's revolutionised aviation navigation, replacing many traditional aids (NDB, VOR) for general aviation.
exam_tip: GNSS: satellite signals give position. At least 4 satellites needed. Trilateration: distance to satellites calculated from signal delay. Very accurate, weather-independent.
key_facts: GNSS: Global Navigation Satellite System (GPS, Galileo, GLONASS, BeiDou)
Satellites: 30+ in orbit, continuous global coverage
Receiver: picks up signals, calculates distances
Trilateration: 4 satellites for 3D position (lat, long, alt)
Accuracy: civil receivers ±5–10 metres typical
Weather independent: signal penetrates clouds, works anywhere
Position update: 1–10 times per second typical
Integration: combined with air data and heading for moving map
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the limitations of the Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS).
concept: GNSS limitations include: signal blockage (the signals are L-band radio and don't penetrate large metal structures like tunnels or heavily forested areas, but they do penetrate aircraft fuselages well enough for navigation). Signal blockage in urban canyons (tall buildings) can cause multipath errors where signals bounce off buildings before reaching the receiver, degrading accuracy. Atmospheric effects (ionospheric delays, tropospheric delays) add small errors (metres) that vary with time of day and solar activity. Satellite geometry matters: if all visible satellites are clustered in one part of the sky, position accuracy is poorer than if they're spread evenly. GNSS signal jamming (intentional or unintentional) can degrade or block the signal. GPS (USA government system) is not guaranteed for all users and could theoretically be degraded or denied for security reasons (though this is rare). No backup: GNSS alone doesn't provide cross-check of accuracy. A bad position calculation might go unnoticed without confirmation from other sources. Loss of signal (such as flying in a canyon with heavy cloud cover) means you lose position until signal is regained. Cold start: when you first power on a GNSS receiver, it takes time (several minutes) to acquire initial position ('cold start' or 'time to first fix'). Warm start (cached satellite position and time) is faster. Accuracy degradation at high latitudes (above 70–75 degrees) due to satellite geometry. For these reasons, aviation regulations require GNSS to be backed up by other navigation aids (VOR, NDB, inertial navigation) for critical applications. For general aviation and backup navigation, GNSS alone is sufficient.
exam_tip: GNSS limitations: signal blockage, multipath (buildings), atmospheric delays, satellite geometry, jamming possible, no inherent backup, cold start delay. Accuracy varies. Always cross-check with other aids.
key_facts: Signal blockage: tunnels/forests block signal
Urban canyons: tall buildings cause multipath errors
Atmospheric effects: ionospheric and tropospheric delays
Satellite geometry: spread of satellites affects accuracy
Jamming: intentional or unintentional can degrade signal
GPS caveat: US government system, availability not guaranteed
No backup: GNSS alone can't verify accuracy
Cold start: initial acquisition takes time (minutes)
High latitude: accuracy degrades above ~75° latitude
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the term RAIM prediction and the significance of these predictions.
concept: RAIM stands for 'Receiver Autonomous Integrity Monitoring'. It's a mathematical function that detects when a GNSS receiver's position solution is unreliable by comparing calculations made using different subsets of available satellites. If you have 5 satellites, the receiver calculates position using satellites 1-4, then using 1-3-4-5, then 1-2-4-5, etc. If all calculations yield the same position (within tolerance), RAIM says the solution is good. If one calculation is significantly different, RAIM detects a problem: one of the satellites might be providing bad data (or you're experiencing multipath/jamming). RAIM prediction is a pre-flight function: you input your desired departure and arrival locations and time of flight, and the GNSS receiver (or a ground-based tool) predicts whether RAIM will be available throughout your planned route and time. RAIM availability depends on satellite geometry: at certain times and locations, there might not be enough satellites with good geometry to provide reliable monitoring. If RAIM is predicted to be unavailable, the receiver warns you before flight. The significance is that RAIM availability determines whether you can safely rely on GNSS for primary navigation. In areas or times with poor satellite geometry, RAIM might not be available, meaning the receiver can't verify its solution. Without RAIM, you're flying GNSS-only without automatic integrity checking, which is considered less safe for critical operations. For general aviation in NZ, RAIM is usually available (good satellite coverage), but in remote areas or at high latitudes, RAIM gaps occur. Pilots planning to rely on GNSS for critical navigation (such as approach) need to verify RAIM availability before flight. Modern integrated systems (glass cockpits) display RAIM status in-flight, warning if RAIM is lost.
exam_tip: RAIM: receiver checks itself using multiple satellite subsets. Detects bad satellite or interference. Prediction: pre-flight check that RAIM will be available. No RAIM = less reliable, use backup aids.
key_facts: RAIM: receiver autonomous integrity monitoring
Method: compares position using different satellite combinations
Detects: bad satellite signal or multipath/jamming effects
RAIM prediction: pre-flight availability check
Depends on: satellite geometry at your location and time
RAIM gaps: occur in some areas or times with poor geometry
Significance: available RAIM = trustworthy position
No RAIM: position less trusted, use backup navigation aids
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the term RNP and the significance of the RNP value.
concept: RNP stands for 'Required Navigation Performance'. It's a specification of how accurately an aircraft must navigate to fly a particular route or procedure. RNP is expressed as a radius in nautical miles: RNP 10 means the aircraft must be within 10 nautical miles (19 km) of the intended track 95% of the time. RNP 5 means within 5 nautical miles 95% of the time. RNP 0.3 means within 0.3 nautical miles (about 550 metres) 95% of the time. Different procedures require different RNP: a standard GNSS approach might require RNP 0.3 to 1.0 (tight accuracy); cruise airways might allow RNP 10. Achieving a specific RNP requires the aircraft's navigation system to be capable, the pilot to be trained, and the RAIM/integrity checking to be available. An aircraft equipped with a modern GNSS receiver and integrated navigation system might achieve RNP 0.3. An older aircraft with only standalone GNSS might only achieve RNP 5. The significance is that RNP defines navigation safety for a given airspace or procedure. If you're flying an RNP 0.3 procedure but your aircraft can only achieve RNP 5, you're not qualified for that procedure and it's not safe. RNP requirements are becoming the standard way to define navigation performance globally, replacing older methods that relied on specific ground-based radio aids. Operators must know their aircraft's certified RNP level and only use it for procedures requiring that RNP or better.
exam_tip: RNP: required navigation performance, expressed in nautical miles. RNP 10 = 10 nm track, RNP 0.3 = 0.3 nm. Aircraft must achieve required RNP for procedure. Know your aircraft's certified RNP.
key_facts: RNP: required navigation performance
Unit: nautical miles radius from intended track
Standard: 95% compliance (5% outside allowed)
RNP 10: cruise, standard procedures, ±10 nm
RNP 5: more demanding airways, better systems
RNP 1: precision approach, modern GNSS
RNP 0.3: tight approach procedures, best equipment
Significance: defines qualification for procedure, ensures safety
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the function and operation of a typical TCAS system.
concept: TCAS stands for 'Traffic Collision Avoidance System'. It's a safety system that detects nearby aircraft and alerts the pilot if a collision is imminent. A typical TCAS has two levels of alerting: Traffic Advisories (TA) and Resolution Advisories (RA). The system uses radar and transponder signals: it sends out interrogations (radar pulses) and listens for transponder replies from other aircraft. By measuring the time for replies and direction of signals, TCAS calculates the range, bearing, and altitude of nearby aircraft. If another aircraft is detected, TCAS estimates its trajectory (where it will be in the next few seconds). If the trajectories will bring the aircraft too close, TCAS generates a TA: an audio alert like 'Traffic' and a visual indication on the display showing the target's location. The pilot then visually acquires the traffic and takes evasive action if needed. If the aircraft are getting even closer and a collision is likely within a few seconds, TCAS generates an RA (Resolution Advisory): a specific command like 'Climb, Climb' or 'Descend, Descend' displayed on the screen and announced aurally. The RA tells the pilot exactly what to do: if it says 'Climb', the pilot pitches up to increase altitude. If it says 'Descend', the pilot pitches down to decrease altitude. TCAS calculates RAs to ensure the manoeuvre will increase separation safely. RAs typically command ±1500–2000 feet of vertical movement. Once separation improves, TCAS cancels the RA. The system is designed so that if both aircraft have TCAS and both follow their RAs, collision is prevented even if RAs are opposite (one climbs, one descends—both move apart). TCAS is standard on transport aircraft and increasingly common on business jets. It requires a working transponder with altitude reporting (Mode C or S).
exam_tip: TCAS: detects aircraft via transponder replies. Sends TA (traffic warning) if close. Sends RA (resolution advisory: climb/descend) if collision imminent. Pilot follows RA immediately. Transponder required.
key_facts: TCAS: traffic collision avoidance system
Detection: radar interrogation + transponder replies
Data: range, bearing, altitude of nearby aircraft
TA (Traffic Advisory): audio/visual alert, pilot acquires visually
RA (Resolution Advisory): specific command (climb/descend)
RA authority: pilot must follow immediately unless unsafe
RA calculation: ensures separation when both follow RA
Standard transport aircraft: TCAS standard equipment
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the function and operation of a typical GPWS system.
concept: GPWS stands for 'Ground Proximity Warning System'. It's a safety system that detects when the aircraft is too close to the ground or terrain and alerts the pilot. A typical GPWS has multiple modes triggered by different unsafe conditions. Mode 1: 'Excessive Descent Rate'—if the aircraft is descending faster than safe for the current altitude and flight path, GPWS sounds an audio warning ('Whoop Whoop Pull Up') and displays a visual alert. Mode 2: 'Terrain Ahead, Pull Up'—GPWS compares the aircraft's altitude against a terrain database (a digital map of ground elevations). If the aircraft is flying toward terrain that's higher than current altitude, GPWS warns. Mode 3: 'Altitude Loss after Takeoff'—if the aircraft loses altitude immediately after takeoff or goes-around, GPWS warns (preventing premature descent). Mode 4: 'Unsafe Terrain Clearance'—if terrain is too close in any direction, GPWS warns. Mode 5: 'Ground Level Wind Shear'—if wind shear is detected during approach (wind pushing aircraft down), GPWS warns. The system uses multiple inputs: pressure altitude (from the air data computer), ground proximity radar or GPS/terrain database (modern GPWS uses GPS and a detailed terrain map), and aircraft configuration (gear and flap position). GPWS logic combines these to determine if a warning is needed. GPWS is designed to be conservative: it errs on the side of over-warning rather than missing a real threat. This means occasional false alarms during unusual manoeuvres (steep terrain, high altitude airport surrounded by higher terrain nearby). Modern GPWS (called Terrain Awareness and Warning System, TAWS) is electronic, uses GPS and terrain databases, and is more accurate and informative than older systems. GPWS/TAWS is standard on transport and business aircraft and increasingly mandated on all aircraft.
exam_tip: GPWS: detects terrain collision threat. Uses altitude, radar, GPS, terrain database. 'Whoop Whoop Pull Up' = descent rate unsafe. 'Terrain Ahead' = ground too high. Pilot must respond immediately.
key_facts: GPWS: ground proximity warning system
Inputs: altitude, radar, GPS, terrain database
Mode 1: excessive descent rate warning
Mode 2: terrain ahead pull up warning
Mode 3: altitude loss after takeoff warning
Mode 4: unsafe terrain clearance warning
Mode 5: wind shear during approach warning
Modern: TAWS (terrain awareness) is electronic, GPS-based
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the components and operation of a typical EFIS cockpit display system.
concept: EFIS stands for 'Electronic Flight Instrument System'. It replaces traditional mechanical instruments with digital displays showing flight information on screens. A typical EFIS cockpit has two main displays: the Primary Flight Display (PFD) and the Navigation Display (ND). The PFD shows attitude (artificial horizon), airspeed, altitude, vertical speed, heading, and other critical flight parameters in a format familiar to pilots (resembling the steam gauges they're used to). The ND (or moving map display) shows the aircraft's position, navigation aids (VORs, NDBs), waypoints, airways, radar weather, and terrain. The EFIS system has several components: data sources (air data computer, heading reference system, navigation sensors, weather radar), processing units (flight management system, air data computer, display processors), and the displays themselves (typically large LCD or digital screens). The air data computer (ADC) processes pitot-static inputs to calculate true airspeed, altitude, and vertical speed. The heading reference system (HRS, also called attitude heading reference system, AHRS) uses accelerometers, gyros, and magnetometers to calculate attitude (pitch and roll) and heading. The flight management system (FMS) integrates all data and provides navigation, flight planning, autopilot commands, and synthesis. The displays are modular: you can have one large screen showing both PFD and ND side-by-side, or separate screens, or smaller auxiliary displays. Modern glass cockpits typically have two main displays (captain and first officer), a centre overhead display, and possibly smaller screens for engine parameters and checklists. The system is incredibly flexible: you can customize what information is displayed and how it's presented. Pilots can select different display modes (approach mode zooms the map, cruise mode shows a wider view) and can select which navigation sources are shown.
exam_tip: EFIS: electronic displays replace steam gauges. Main displays: PFD (attitude, speed, alt, heading), ND (moving map, navaids, weather). Sources: ADC (air data), AHRS (attitude/heading), FMS (integration). Flexible, customizable.
key_facts: EFIS: electronic flight instrument system
PFD: primary flight display (attitude, airspeed, altitude)
ND: navigation display (moving map, navaids, terrain)
ADC: air data computer (airspeed, altitude, VSI)
AHRS: attitude heading reference system (pitch, roll, heading)
FMS: flight management system (integration, planning, autopilot)
Data sources: pitot-static, attitude sensors, heading sensors, GPS, weather radar
Display: modular, can show PFD, ND, engine info, checklists
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the function of the: (a) air data computer; (b) signal generator; (c) input data sources to a basic glass flight display system.
concept: The air data computer (ADC) is a processor that takes inputs from the pitot-static system and calculates airspeed, altitude, and vertical speed for display. It receives pitot pressure (total pressure) and static pressure from the pitot-static system, applies corrections for instrument and position error, temperature compensation, and outputs true airspeed, indicated airspeed, altitude, and vertical speed. The ADC also calculates Mach number (true airspeed relative to speed of sound), which is important at high altitude. The signal generator (or synchro generator) is a component that converts aircraft parameters (like heading or altitude) into electrical signals for transmission to displays or other systems. Some older systems use analog synchro signals; modern systems use digital signals. In a basic glass flight display system, the signal generator might convert heading from the AHRS into a signal sent to the display. Input data sources to a basic glass system include: the air data computer (airspeed, altitude, VSI, Mach), the AHRS (pitch, roll, heading), the autopilot (mode, guidance), transponder data (altitude, identity), and optionally GNSS (position, ground speed, track), weather radar (weather, precipitation), and terrain database (terrain height). The display integrates all these inputs and presents them in a coherent picture to the pilot. The system architecture is modular: the air data computer sends airspeed and altitude data on a data bus; the AHRS sends attitude and heading on the same bus; the display reads from the bus and formats everything for presentation. This allows flexibility: if one system fails, others can continue to function, and the display can show degraded information (e.g., attitude estimated from autopilot if AHRS fails) or alerts the pilot to the failure.
exam_tip: ADC: processes pitot-static, outputs airspeed/altitude/VSI. Signal generator: converts parameters to signals. Data sources: ADC, AHRS, autopilot, transponder, GNSS, weather radar. Integration provides complete display.
key_facts: Air Data Computer: pitot-static input → airspeed, altitude, VSI
ADC calculations: instrument error, position error, temperature compensation
ADC outputs: IAS, TAS, altitude, VSI, Mach number
Signal generator: converts parameters to electrical signals
Data sources: ADC (air data), AHRS (attitude/heading), autopilot, transponder
Optional sources: GNSS (position), weather radar, terrain database
Integration: all sources combined on data bus for display
Modularity: system continues if one component fails
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the components and operation of an Attitude Heading Reference System (AHRS).
concept: An AHRS (Attitude Heading Reference System) is a self-contained system that calculates aircraft attitude (pitch and roll) and heading using internal sensors and no external references. The main components are: accelerometers (measure acceleration in three axes), gyroscopes (measure rotation rates), and a magnetometer (measure Earth's magnetic field). The AHRS processes signals from these sensors using complex algorithms to calculate aircraft pitch, roll, and heading continuously. The accelerometers sense gravity and aircraft acceleration; the gyros sense rotation rates; the magnetometer provides heading reference. The algorithm fuses all three sensor inputs to produce accurate attitude and heading estimates. The fusion is necessary because individual sensors have errors: gyros drift over time, accelerometers are noisy and affected by aircraft acceleration, magnetometers are affected by local magnetic disturbances (aircraft structure, electrical fields). By combining all three, the AHRS compensates for individual sensor weaknesses. The AHRS outputs pitch, roll, and heading (and optionally turn rate, slip/skid) to the flight displays and autopilot. The system is independent of any external references: it doesn't need vacuum (like a mechanical gyro), doesn't need compass swing calibration, and works in any attitude including inverted flight. The disadvantage is it depends on electrical power: no power means no AHRS. Also, the system can 'drift' (attitude error increases over time) if the sensors develop faults or if the fusion algorithm is confused (such as during a spin or unusual manoeuvre where acceleration doesn't match the calculated attitude). Modern AHRS systems are very accurate (±1–2 degrees pitch/roll) and reliable, and they're standard in all modern glass cockpits.
exam_tip: AHRS: accelerometers + gyros + magnetometer + fusion algorithm = attitude and heading. No external reference needed. Accurate and independent. Requires electrical power.
key_facts: AHRS: attitude heading reference system
Sensors: accelerometers (3-axis), gyroscopes (rotation rates), magnetometer (heading)
Algorithm: sensor fusion combining all three inputs
Outputs: pitch, roll, heading, turn rate, slip/skid
Advantages: independent, works in any attitude, accurate, reliable
Disadvantage: requires electrical power
Drift: attitude error can accumulate if sensors fault or in unusual manoeuvres
Modern accuracy: ±1–2 degrees pitch and roll typical
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Describe the management of EFIS system failures.
concept: EFIS system failures can range from minor (loss of one display) to major (complete system failure). The system is designed with redundancy to manage failures gracefully. A typical modern cockpit has two PFDs (captain and first officer) and optionally a centre display. If one PFD fails, the pilot can transition to the other display and continue flying (though it's more cluttered because you're now trying to see everything from one screen). If both PFDs fail, the pilot must transition to standby mechanical instruments (if equipped) or hand-fly using the magnetic compass and basic flight instruments. The AHRS is critical: if it fails, the displays show 'flag' (a visual indication that the data is invalid), and the autopilot disengages. The pilot can continue hand-flying if trained to do so, relying on the magnetic compass for heading. The air data computer provides airspeed and altitude: if it fails, the displays flag airspeed and altitude, and the pilot must manage speed and altitude using pitch and power control without instrument reference. Modern systems have redundant data paths: the PFD and autopilot receive data from both the ADC and AHRS, so if one fails, the other can provide backup (though less precisely). Weather radar failure is inconvenient but non-critical: the display shows no weather, but the aircraft can continue safely. GNSS failure: the ND shows no GPS position, but the pilot can navigate using other aids or track visually. The system logic is designed so that a single failure doesn't cascade: a failed AHRS doesn't prevent the ADC from working, a failed display doesn't prevent the autopilot from functioning (though it might operate degraded). Most significant is loss of both primary flight data sources (AHRS and ADC together): in that case, the aircraft relies on manual flying and basic mechanical standby instruments. For this reason, regulations often require standby instruments (mechanical AI, altimeter, airspeed) in aircraft relying on EFIS as their primary instruments.
exam_tip: EFIS failures: system designed with redundancy. Single failure: transition to backup display or standby instruments. Dual failure: hand-fly with compass. Know your aircraft's backup strategy.
key_facts: Redundancy: dual PFDs, dual ADC/AHRS common in modern aircraft
Single display failure: transition to other display
AHRS failure: autopilot disengages, displays flag, hand-fly with compass
ADC failure: airspeed/altitude lost, manage using pitch and power
System logic: failures don't cascade
Standby instruments: mechanical AI, altimeter, airspeed required by regulation
Graceful degradation: system designed to fail safely
Training: pilots must be proficient in hand-flying if EFIS fails
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge, ATC AGK

syllabus_desc: Explain the reasons why excessively high engine temperatures and rapid cooling must be avoided.
concept: Your engine's temperature management is all about keeping things in that sweet spot. If the cylinders get too hot, you're looking at detonation, pre-ignition, and your piston rings seizing. Cool things down too fast though—say, a rapid descent after climb—and you've got thermal shock cracking your cylinders. You want smooth, gradual temperature changes.
exam_tip: They'll test whether you understand detonation risk at high temps and thermal shock at low temps. Know that slow cooling is better than rapid cooling.
key_facts: Excessive heat causes detonation, pre-ignition, and component failure
Rapid cooling causes thermal shock and cylinder cracking
Maintain steady power settings to avoid temperature swings
Cowl flaps and airflow management control temperature rise and descent rate
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Describe the ways in which heat from the combustion process is dispersed from a typical aero-engine.
concept: Most of the heat energy from combustion gets wasted anyway—only about 20-30% actually goes into useful power. The rest goes three ways: through the exhaust gases (takes away heaps of heat), through the engine oil (which circulates and picks up heat), and through the cylinder cooling fins into the airstream. That's why we have oil coolers and baffles directing air across those fins.
exam_tip: Remember the three main heat dispersal paths: exhaust, oil circulation, and direct cooling fins. The oil's your main cooling agent inside the engine.
key_facts: Only 20-30% of combustion heat converts to useful power
Heat dispersed via exhaust gases, engine oil circulation, and cylinder cooling fins
Oil cooler removes heat from oil before it returns to sump
Cowling baffles direct air flow over cooling fins
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Lycoming/Textron technical data

syllabus_desc: In a typical aircraft engine, explain the function of: (a) cowling ducts and baffles; (b) cooling fins; (c) pilot-operated cowl flaps; (d) oil cooler; (e) radiator; (f) intercooler.
concept: Think of your cooling system like directing a river of air. The cowling ducts and baffles are your channels—they force cold air to flow where you need it. Cooling fins are just extra surface area bolted to each cylinder; more surface means more heat transfer. Cowl flaps are your throttle for cooling; you open them if you're hot, close them if you need to climb and temperature's good. The oil cooler's like a radiator but for oil—cooling it before it recirculates. Radiators cool fuel and other systems. Intercoolers cool supercharged air between stages before it goes to the cylinders, which lets you run higher boost pressure.
exam_tip: Cowl flaps are pilot-operated—you control them. Oil cooler keeps oil in the sweet spot. Intercoolers let you squeeze more boost pressure safely.
key_facts: Cowling ducts and baffles direct ram air over cooling fins
Cooling fins increase surface area for heat dissipation
Cowl flaps (pilot-operated) control airflow and engine temperature
Oil cooler removes heat from engine oil circulation
Radiator cools fuel and secondary systems
Intercooler cools compressed air from supercharger to allow higher boost pressure
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Bob Tait CPL Gen Knowledge

syllabus_desc: Explain the general engine handling techniques for maintaining engine temperatures within the proper range, for aircraft: (a) without CHT gauge or cowl flaps; (b) fitted with CHT gauge and cowl flaps.
concept: If you've got an older airframe without a CHT gauge, you're managing temperature by controlling power, descent rate, and airspeed. Keep power reasonable, descend slowly, and use cruise descent techniques rather than diving. If you've got a modern twin with CHT and cowl flaps, you've got precision tools. Monitor your CHTs, open cowl flaps if any cylinder starts creeping high, and close them once you've got good airflow and climb performance back. Before takeoff, exercise those cowl flaps to make sure they move freely—you don't want them stuck.
exam_tip: Without CHT: manage via power and descent rate. With CHT and cowl flaps: monitor continuously and adjust cowl flaps as needed. Always exercise cowl flaps before flight.
key_facts: Without CHT/cowl flaps: manage via power settings, descent rate, and airspeed
With CHT and cowl flaps: monitor CHT continuously and adjust flap position
Cowl flaps should be exercised on ground before takeoff
Descend at cruise descent rate (200 fpm rule) to avoid rapid cooling
Close cowl flaps for takeoff to build airflow and climb performance
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, AFM/POH procedures

syllabus_desc: Explain the requirements which the undercarriage/landing system must be able to meet.
concept: Your undercarriage's a mechanical system that's got to absorb all the energy when you land—vertical loads, side loads, braking loads. It's got to be strong enough for maximum landing weight and rough fields, handle side loading if you touch down with a drift, absorb braking energy without collapsing, and be light enough you're not carrying dead weight around the sky. The gear needs to isolate vibration from the airframe, fold up neatly if it's retractable, and keep steering responsive on the ground.
exam_tip: Remember: strength, side-load capability, braking absorption, weight efficiency, and vibration isolation. CPL examiners love the structural design aspects.
key_facts: Must absorb vertical landing loads at maximum landing weight
Must handle side loads from drift landings and crosswind operations
Must absorb braking energy without structural failure
Must be as light as possible to minimise weight penalty
Must isolate airframe from ground vibration
Must provide adequate steering control on ground
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, JAA PPL/CPL syllabus

syllabus_desc: Describe the features of a typical undercarriage construction.
concept: A fixed gear setup on a light twin is pretty straightforward. You've got a main leg on each side and a nose leg, all bolted to the fuselage or wing structure at stress points. Each leg's got an oleo-pneumatic shock strut—that's spring air and damping oil working together. At the bottom you'll find a wheel with brake assembly, tyre, and a spat or fairings to cut drag. The nose leg's connected to the rudder pedals for steering through a mechanical linkage. Everything's designed to flex and absorb energy without transmitting shock up into the airframe.
exam_tip: Fixed gear means main plus nose gear configuration. Know the oleo-pneumatic strut is your shock absorber. Nose wheel steering's mechanical through the pedals.
key_facts: Main landing gear legs mounted symmetrically, nose gear forward
Oleo-pneumatic shock struts provide spring and damping
Each wheel assembly includes brake, tyre, and hub
Nose wheel connected to rudder pedals for steering control
Fairings and wheel pants minimise drag
Gear legs bolted at structural stress points on fuselage/wing
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Piper/Beechcraft technical manuals

syllabus_desc: Explain the operation of an oleo-pneumatic shock strut.
concept: An oleo strut's basically a hydraulic shock absorber with built-in springs. You've got a cylinder with rod and piston inside. The upper part contains pressurised nitrogen gas—that's your spring. Below the piston is oil with a metering orifice. When you land and the wheel compresses upward, the piston moves down, squeezing the oil through that tiny hole (metering orifice). The nitrogen gets compressed, absorbing energy like a spring. As the wheel rebounds, the oil flows back through the orifice, with the metering hole controlling the speed—that's your damping. Without that orifice you'd bounce all day.
exam_tip: Nitrogen = spring action, oil = damping medium, metering orifice = controls rebound speed. A bouncy landing means the orifice might be partly blocked.
key_facts: Nitrogen gas provides spring (elastic) force
Oil provides damping to absorb rebound energy
Metering orifice controls rate of oil flow during rebound
Compression strokes (landing) are faster than rebound strokes (slower)
Pressure increases with compression, nitrogen compliance provides shock absorption
Over-servicing with air reduces oil capacity and damping effectiveness
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Akron Aerostructure technical data

syllabus_desc: Describe the pilot checks of oleo-pneumatic undercarriage legs.
concept: Before flight you're checking the health of those struts. Get down there with a tyre gauge and check the air pressure—your POH's got the numbers. For a Piper Aztec that's usually around 170 psi, but check your specific aircraft. Press on the strut; it should feel firm with a small amount of give—maybe 2-3 cm compression. If it's too soft, you've either lost air pressure or the metering orifice is blocked. If it's hard as concrete, you might be over-pressurised. Look for oil seepage around the piston rod and at the rod wiper seal. Some light weeping's normal; heavy oil trails mean it's time for maintenance. Check the tyres for tread, wear, and condition.
exam_tip: Air pressure check is critical. Soft struts affect both landing characteristics and braking effectiveness. Know the aircraft's pressure limits.
key_facts: Check nitrogen pressure against AFM/POH specification
Strut should compress 2-3 cm when pressed by hand
Soft strut indicates low pressure or blocked metering orifice
Hard strut indicates over-pressurisation
Inspect for oil seepage around piston rod
Check tyre tread, wear, and condition
Rod wiper seal should show no excessive leakage
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Piper Cherokee/Aztec POH

syllabus_desc: Describe the construction of aircraft wheel assemblies and tyres.
concept: Your aircraft wheel's a two-part assembly. You've got a wheel rim (usually aluminium alloy) with the tyre mounted on it, and it spins on a stub axle. The axle comes out the end of the landing gear leg and's held in place by a castellated nut with a cotter pin—you'll see that during preflight. Inside the wheel cavity you've got bearing races (usually ball or tapered roller bearings) packed with grease. The brake disc bolts to the wheel, and when you press the pedal, the brake assembly clamps that disc. Aircraft tyres are radial ply construction (not bias), rated for specific load and speed limits. They're beefier than car tyres because we land hard and take off quick. Tread depth matters for water drainage on wet strips.
exam_tip: Two-piece assembly: rim and tyre. Bearings are sealed and packed. Radial ply tyres are standard. Know that tyre failure is rare but brake heat can damage it.
key_facts: Wheel assembly consists of rim (aluminium alloy) and tyre
Wheel spins on stub axle secured with castellated nut and cotter pin
Ball or tapered roller bearings packed with grease
Brake disc bolts directly to wheel
Aircraft tyres are radial ply construction
Load and speed ratings specified for each tyre type
Tread depth important for wet runway water drainage
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Goodyear/Michelin aviation tyre technical data

syllabus_desc: Explain the requirements for tyre care and checks.
concept: Your tyres are wearing out every flight—it's just a question of how much. Before flight, visually inspect for tread depth, cracks, separation, and uneven wear. Minimum tread is usually 2/32 inch depth; if you're much below that, you're risking hydroplaning on wet strips. Check for cuts, stones lodged in treads, and any bulges in the sidewall (that's a flat). Look at the tyre for even wear across the width—one-sided wear might mean your wheel alignment's off. After landing, feel the tyres gently (careful, they can be hot). If one's noticeably hotter than the others, something's wrong—maybe brake drag or under-inflation. Check pressure cold (preferably next morning) because hot-pressure readings are misleading. Store tyres indoors, out of sunlight and away from ozone-generating equipment. Ultraviolet light and ozone destroy rubber.
exam_tip: Minimum 2/32 inch tread. Check pressure cold. Uneven wear or hot spots mean investigation needed. UV and ozone are your enemies.
key_facts: Minimum tread depth 2/32 inch (0.6 mm)
Inspect visually before every flight for cracks, separation, and bulges
Check for even wear across tyre width
Feel tyres after landing—excessive heat indicates problems
Measure tyre pressure cold, not after landing
Correct inflation according to AFM/POH specification
Store tyres indoors, protected from UV and ozone
Rotate tyres per maintenance schedule to extend life
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, FAA Advisory Circulars, Goodyear/Michelin guidelines

syllabus_desc: Describe the essential features of a retractable undercarriage system.
concept: A retractable gear system's got to be reliable—you can't have it failing mid-flight. You've got hydraulic actuators (cylinders with rods) that extend and retract the gear legs, rotating them into the wheel wells. The system includes a pump (engine-driven), selector valve (your control in the cockpit), check valves to prevent backflow, sequencing valves to extend the legs in the right order, and an accumulator to provide emergency extend pressure if the engine pump fails. There's also a gear-position indicating system—usually micro-switches on each leg that tell you green/up/down. Landing gear warning systems alert you if you're trying to land with the gear up. It's all redundant and safety-critical because a gear-up landing ruins the entire aircraft.
exam_tip: Hydraulic system with sequencing, position indication, and warning systems. Know that the accumulator's your emergency backup. Gear extension is gravity-assisted; retraction is powered.
key_facts: Engine-driven hydraulic pump provides actuation power
Selector valve in cockpit controls extend/retract function
Hydraulic actuators (cylinders) rotate gear legs
Accumulator stores pressure for emergency manual extension
Sequencing valves ensure correct extend/retract order
Micro-switches on each leg provide position indication
Gear-position lights (green=down/locked, red=unsafe, off=up/locked)
Warning horn/light activates if landing is attempted with gear up
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Piper/Beechcraft systems manuals

syllabus_desc: Explain the speed limitations for landing gear operation.
concept: You can't operate retractable gear at any speed you fancy. There's a maximum gear-extension speed (usually marked VLO on your airspeed indicator—that's 'Velocity, Landing gear Operating'). Exceed that and air loads can prevent the gear from fully extending, or the gear can deploy partially and cause a partial collapse, which is catastrophic. Usually VLO's around 120-150 knots depending on the aircraft. There's also a gear-lowering speed (typically lower than VLO) because airflow over partially-extended gear causes drag and noise. Some aircraft have gear-limiting blowout doors that open if you exceed limits, protecting the system. Once the gear's fully down and locked, you can land from any speed down to your stall speed, but ideally you want to be above the gear-cycling speed just in case you need to go around.
exam_tip: VLO is your critical limit. Exceeding it can prevent gear extension. Some aircraft have speed checks every 1000 feet on descent to keep you legal.
key_facts: VLO (Velocity, Landing gear Operating) is maximum gear extension/retraction speed
VLO typically 120-150 knots depending on aircraft type
Gear-lowering speed may be lower than VLO depending on design
Exceeding VLO can prevent full extension or cause partial collapse
Once gear fully down and locked, landing speed limited only by stall speed
Blowout doors on some gear systems protect against overspeed deployment
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, AFM/POH airspeed limitations

syllabus_desc: Describe typical: (a) emergency gear operation systems; (b) associated emergency gear operation procedures.
concept: If your hydraulic system fails and the gear won't come down, you've got emergency systems. Most aircraft have a manual crank or handpump in the cockpit—you crank it manually to lower the gear (takes effort, but it works). Some bigger aircraft have a pneumatic emergency system using compressed air stored in an accumulator. When you actuate the emergency system, it pushes hydraulic pressure or air to the actuators, extending the gear through gravity-assist. Your emergency procedure involves slowing to below VLO, heading for an airfield with good crash equipment on standby, declaring the emergency to ATC, and deploying the gear manually. You'll check the gear-down lights before attempting landing. If you don't get three green lights, you've got options: gear-up landing on grass if available, or belly landing on a prepared strip with emergency services standing by.
exam_tip: Manual hand-pump or pneumatic accumulator are common emergency systems. Slow down, declare emergency, attempt manual extension, confirm three greens, then land. Know your aircraft's specific procedure.
key_facts: Manual handpump is most common emergency extension method
Pneumatic accumulator provides air pressure for emergency actuation
Manual extension available below VLO speed
Procedure: slow down, declare emergency with ATC, attempt manual extension
Confirm three green lights (or equivalent) before landing
If emergency extension fails, gear-up landing on grass preferred to belly landing
Grass fields at Ardmore, Hamilton, Christchurch suitable for emergency landing
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Piper/Beechcraft emergency procedures

syllabus_desc: Describe typical gear warning and indicating systems.
concept: Your gear indication system uses micro-switches on each landing gear leg. When the leg's fully extended and locked, the switch closes and completes a circuit to your green light (or green needle on an indicator). When the leg's fully retracted, a different switch closes, and the light goes out—that's your 'up and locked' condition. Any intermediate position (partially extended) leaves both switches open, and you get a red light or flashing warning. The warning horn's usually wired to the throttle proximity switch; if you reduce power (like on approach) and the gear's still up, a horn blares in the cockpit. Some systems have redundant indicator lamps. The beauty of micro-switches is they're simple, reliable, and require no pilot input—they just tell you what the gear's actually doing.
exam_tip: Green = down and locked on all three legs. Red or no light = unsafe condition. Warning horn sounds if throttle is reduced with gear up.
key_facts: Micro-switches on each landing gear leg sense position
Green light indicates gear fully extended and locked
Red light indicates unsafe condition (partial extension)
No light indicates gear fully retracted and locked
Warning horn activates when power is reduced with gear in unsafe position
Redundant indication systems on some aircraft types
Indicators are independent of hydraulic system functionality
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, electrical systems manuals

syllabus_desc: Describe the function and operation of ground retraction protection systems.
concept: You're sitting on the ground at Ardmore after landing, and you don't want someone accidentally raising the gear while you're still on the pavement—you'd break the gear legs and wreck the aircraft. Ground retraction protection systems prevent this. The usual method is a squat switch on one or both main landing gear legs. When the aircraft is on the ground and weight's on the legs, this switch closes. If someone tries to operate the gear selector, the squat switch blocks the 'up' command from reaching the hydraulic system—the selector moves but nothing happens. The system releases once the aircraft's airborne and weight comes off the legs (the switch opens). Some systems also use lock-out solenoids that are energised by ground power or a separate circuit. The key is that the squat switch is purely mechanical with no pilot action required—it's a passive safety system.
exam_tip: Squat switch prevents accidental gear retraction on the ground. Automatically released in flight. Know that on some aircraft you manually reset ground protection after landing.
key_facts: Squat switch mounted on main landing gear leg(s)
Switch closes when aircraft weight is on the landing gear
Closed squat switch blocks 'up' command to hydraulic system
Switch opens and protection releases when aircraft becomes airborne
Prevents inadvertent gear retraction on the ground
System is automatic—no pilot action required
Some aircraft require manual reset of ground-sensing system
source: NZ CAA AC 61-5, Piper/Beechcraft systems manuals

syllabus_desc: Describe the principle of operation of the common types of wheel brake units.
concept: Aircraft brakes are basically disc brakes. You've got a rotating brake disc that's bolted to the wheel, and a stationary brake assembly (caliper) mounted on the landing gear leg. Inside the caliper are friction pads and actuating pistons. When you push the brake pedal, hydraulic pressure flows into the caliper, pushing the pistons against the brake pads.
